      "excerpts": [
        "## Navigation Menu\n\nToggle navigation\n\nAppearance settings\n\nSearch or jump to...\n\n# Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...\n\nAppearance settings\n\nResetting focus\n\nagl / **jbig2enc** Public\n\n* Notifications You must be signed in to change notification settings\n* Fork 13\n* Star 67\n\n# agl/jbig2enc\n\nmaster\n\nBranches Tags\n\n \n\nGo to file\n\nCode\n\nOpen more actions menu\n\n## Folders and files\n\n|Name |Name |Last commit message |Last commit date |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n|## Latest commit\n\n## History\n\n[171 Commits](https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc/commits/master/)\n\n 171 Commits |\n|.github/ workflows |.github/ workflows | | |\n|doc |doc | | |\n|images |images | | |\n|meson-support |meson-support | | |\n|src |src | | |\n|subprojects |subprojects | | |\n|.clang-format |.clang-format | | |\n|.gitignore |.gitignore | | |\n|.pre-commit-config.yaml |.pre-commit-config.yaml | | |\n|AUTHORS |AUTHORS | | |\n|CMakeLists.txt |CMakeLists.txt | | |\n|COPYING |COPYING | | |\n|ChangeLog |ChangeLog | | |\n|FORMATTING\\_STANDARD.md |FORMATTING\\_STANDARD.md | | |\n|INSTALL |INSTALL | | |\n|Makefile.am |Makefile.am | | |\n|NEWS |NEWS | | |\n|README.md |README.md | | |\n|VERSION |VERSION | | |\n|autogen.sh |autogen.sh | | |\n|configure.ac |configure.ac | | |\n|jbig2topdf.py |jbig2topdf.py | | |\n|meson.build |meson.build | | |\n|View all files |\n\n## Repository files navigation\n\n* README\n* License\n\n# JBIG2 Encoder\n\n[autotools](https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc/actions/workflows/autotools.yaml) [Msys2](https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc/actions/workflows/msys2.yaml) [meson](https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc/actions/workflows/meson.yaml) [cmake](https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc/actions/workflows/cmake.yaml)\n\nThis is an encoder for JBIG2 .\n\nJBIG2 encodes bi-level (1 bpp) images using a number of clever tricks to get\nbetter compression than G4. This encoder can:\n\n* Generate JBIG2 files, or fragments for embedding in PDFs\n* Generic region encoding\n* Perform symbol extraction, classification and text region coding\n* Perform refinement coding and,\n* Compress multipage documents\n\nIt uses the (Apache-ish licensed) [Leptonica library](https://github.com/danbloomberg/leptonica) . Version 1.74 or later is required.\n\n## Known bugs\n\nThe refinement coding causes Acrobat to crash. It's not known if this is a bug\nin Acrobat, though it may well be.\n\n## Usage\n\n_Note_ : Windows Command Prompt does not support wildcard expansion, so `*.jpg` will not work. You'll need to manually expand the file names yourself or you need to use the latest git code and [MSVC build](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-language/expanding-wildcard-arguments) .\n\nSee the `jbig2enc.h` header for the high level API, or the `jbig2` program for an\nexample of usage:\n\n```\njbig2 -s -a -p -v * .jpg && python3 jbig2topdf.py output > out.pdf\n```\n\nor with standalone mode:\n\n```\njbig2 -a -p -v images/feyn.tif > feyn.jb2 && python3 jbig2topdf.py -s feyn.jb2 > feyn.pdf\n```\n\nto encode jbig2 files for pdf creation.\nIf you want to encode an image and then view output first to include in pdf\n\n```\njbig2 -s -S -p -v -O out.png * .jpg\n```\n\nIf you want to encode an image as jbig2 (can be view in [STDU Viewer](http://www.stdutility.com/stduviewer.html) on Windows) run:\n\n```\njbig2 -s images/feyn.tif > feyn.jb2\n```\n\n### Links\n\n* [jbig2enc-samples](https://github.com/zdenop/jbig2enc-samples)\n* [jbig2enc-minidjvu](https://github.com/ImageProcessing-ElectronicPublications/jbig2enc-minidjvu)\n\n## About\n\nJBIG2 Encoder\n\n### Resources\n\nReadme\n\n### License\n\nView license\n\nActivity\n\n### Stars\n\n**67** stars\n\n### Watchers\n\n**4** watching\n\n### Forks\n\n**13** forks\n\nReport repository\n\n## Releases 3\n\n0\\.31 Latest Apr 21, 2026\n\n\\+ 2 releases\n\n## Packages\n\n## Contributors\n\n* \n* \n*\n\n## Languages\n\n* C++ 75\\.3%\n* C 8\\.7%\n* Python 5\\.5%\n* CMake 3\\.4%\n* Meson 3\\.1%\n* M4 2\\.2%\n* Other 1\\.8%\n\n## Footer"
      ]
    },
    {
      "url": "https://ocrmypdf.readthedocs.io/en/v11.6.2/jbig2.html",
      "title": "Installing the JBIG2 encoder — ocrmypdf 11.6.2 documentation",
      "publish_date": null,
      "excerpts": [
        "<!DOCTYPE html> \n\n* [Docs](https://ocrmypdf.readthedocs.io/en/v11.6.2/index.html) »\n* Installing the JBIG2 encoder\n* [ Edit on GitHub](https://github.com/jbarlow83/OCRmyPDF/blob/v11.6.2/docs/jbig2.rst)\n\n---\n\n# Installing the JBIG2 encoder[¶](#installing-the-jbig2-encoder \"Permalink to this headline\")\n\nMost Linux distributions do not include a JBIG2 encoder since JBIG2 encoding was patented for a long time. All known JBIG2 US patents have expired as of 2017, but it is possible that unknown patents exist.\n\nJBIG2 encoding is recommended for OCRmyPDF and is used to losslessly create smaller PDFs. If JBIG2 encoding not available, lower quality encodings will be used.\n\nJBIG2 decoding is not patented and is performed automatically by most PDF viewers. It is widely supported has been part of the PDF specification since 2001.\n\nOn macOS, Homebrew packages jbig2enc and OCRmyPDF includes it by default. The Docker image for OCRmyPDF also builds its own JBIG2 encoder from source.\nFor all other Linux, you must build a JBIG2 encoder from source:\n\ngit clone https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc\ncd jbig2enc\n./autogen.sh\n./configure && make\n[sudo] make install\n\n## Lossy mode JBIG2[¶](#lossy-mode-jbig2 \"Permalink to this headline\")\n\nOCRmyPDF provides lossy mode JBIG2 as an advanced feature. Users should [review the technical concerns with JBIG2 in lossy mode](https://abbyy.technology/en:kb:tip:jbig2%5Fcompression%5Fand%5Focr)and decide if this feature is acceptable for their use case.\n\nJBIG2 lossy mode does achieve higher compression ratios than any other monochrome (bitonal) compression technology; for large text documents the savings are considerable. JBIG2 lossless still gives great compression ratios and is a major improvement over the older CCITT G4 standard. As explained above, there is some risk of substitution errors.\n\nTo turn on JBIG2 lossy mode, add the argument `--jbig2-lossy`. `--optimize {1,2,3}` are necessary for the argument to take effect also required.\nAlso, a JBIG2 encoder must be installed as described in the previous section.\n\n_Due to an oversight, ocrmypdf v7.0 and v7.1 used lossy mode by default._"
      ]
    },
    {
      "url": "https://ocrmypdf.readthedocs.io/en/v15.4.3/jbig2.html",
      "title": "Installing the JBIG2 encoder — ocrmypdf 15.4.3 documentation",
      "publish_date": null,
      "excerpts": [
        "ocrmypdf\n\n* Introduction\n* Release notes\n* Installing OCRmyPDF\n* Installing additional language packs\n* Installing the JBIG2 encoder\n  \n    + Lossy mode JBIG2\n\nUsage\n\n* Cookbook\n* PDF optimization\n* OCRmyPDF Docker image\n* Advanced features\n* Batch processing\n* Online deployments\n* Performance\n* PDF security issues\n* Common error messages\n\nDevelopers\n\n* Using the OCRmyPDF API\n* Plugins\n* API reference\n* Design notes\n* Contributing guidelines\n* Maintainer notes\n\nocrmypdf\n\n* \n* Installing the JBIG2 encoder\n* [Edit on GitHub](https://github.com/jbarlow83/OCRmyPDF/blob/v15.4.3/docs/jbig2.rst)\n\n* * *\n\n# Installing the JBIG2 encoder ¶\n\nMost Linux distributions do not include a JBIG2 encoder since JBIG2\nencoding was patented for a long time. All known JBIG2 US patents have\nexpired as of 2017, but it is possible that unknown patents exist.\n\nJBIG2 encoding is recommended for OCRmyPDF and is used to losslessly\ncreate smaller PDFs. If JBIG2 encoding is not available, lower quality\nCCITT encoding will be used for monochrome images.\n\nJBIG2 decoding is not patented and is performed automatically by most\nPDF viewers. It is widely supported and has been part of the PDF\nspecification since 2001.\n\nJBIG encoding is automatically provided by these OCRmyPDF packages:\n\\- Docker image (both Ubuntu and Alpine)\n\\- Snap package\n\\- ArchLinux AUR package\n\\- Alpine Linux package\n\\- Homebrew on macOS\n\nFor all other platforms, you would need to build the JBIG2 encoder from source:\n\n```\ngit clone https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc\n cd jbig2enc\n./autogen.sh\n./configure && make\n [ sudo ] make install\n```\n\nDependencies include libtoolize and libleptonica, which on Ubuntu systems\nare packaged as libtool and libleptonica-dev. On Fedora (35) they are packaged\nas libtool and leptonica-devel.\n\n## Lossy mode JBIG2 ¶\n\nOCRmyPDF provides lossy mode JBIG2 as an advanced and potentially dangerous\nfeature. Users should [review the technical concerns with JBIG2 in lossy mode](https://en.wikipedia.\norg/wiki/JBIG2) and decide if this feature is acceptable for their use case. In general,\nthis mode should not be used for archival purposes, should not be used when\nthe original document is not available or will be destroyed, and should\nnot be used when numbers present in the document are important, because\nthere is a risk of 6/8 and 8/6 substitution errors.\n\nJBIG2 lossy mode does achieve higher compression ratios than any other\nmonochrome (bitonal) compression technology; for large text documents\nthe savings are considerable. JBIG2 lossless still gives great\ncompression ratios and is a major improvement over the older CCITT G4\nstandard.\n\nTo turn on JBIG2 lossy mode, add the argument `--jbig2-lossy` . `--optimize {1,2,3}` are necessary for the argument to take effect\nalso required. Also, a JBIG2 encoder must be installed as described in\nthe previous section.\n\nYou can adjust the threshold for JBIG2 compression with the `--jbig2-threshold` . The default is 0.\n85, meaning that if two symbols\nare 85% similar, they will be compressed together.\n\n_Due to an oversight, ocrmypdf v7.0 and v7.1 used lossy mode by default._\n\nPrevious Next\n\n* * *\n\nBuilt with [Sphinx](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/) using a [theme](https://github.com/readthedocs/sphinx_rtd_theme) provided by [Read the Docs](https://readthedocs.org) ."
      ]
    }

        0.32
      
        Latest
          
              

          
      
    

    
      Latest
    
        
          

        
  
  
    

  


        Development & Build System

Require C++17 standard: Modernize the codebase and leverage modern C++ features for safer and more maintainable code.
Improve build systems and GitHub Actions: Renovate the build infrastructure to support multiple build systems (CMake, Meson, autotools) and enhance CI/CD workflows for better reliability and test coverage.

Code Quality & Fixes

Add argument checks: Implement stricter validation of command-line arguments to prevent undefined behavior and improve user feedback.
Replace asprintf with a portable solution: Replace a non-standard glibc function with a cross-platform alternative, improving compatibility with different operating systems (e.g., Windows, BSD).
Fix memory leaks: Resolve several identified memory leaks, including:

An issue in unite_templates() where incorrect reference counting caused memory to not be freed.
Ensuring proper destruction of image data (pixl) in error paths.


Fix forcing DPI (-D switch): Correct a bug where the -D command-line option to set the DPI for images without embedded DPI information was not functioning properly.
Fix undefined behavior: Resolve an undefined bit-shifting operation in the arithmetic coding component (jbig2arith.cc), ensuring deterministic and correct behavior across all compilers and architectures.

Maintenance & Cleanup

Improve resource management: Free resources even when running in non-symbol mode, preventing leaks in all execution paths.

        What's Changed

add version info about Leptonica and available image libs by @zdenop in #109
improve installation instruction and configure test for leptonica by @zdenop in #124
Leakage and undefined behavior fixes by @mia-0 in #125
Meson build system by @mia-0 in #126
Meson: update leptonica wrap to 1.87.0 by @mia-0 in #127
CI/meson: avoid unnecessary WINE binfmt wrapper by @mia-0 in #130
Fix Memory leak in unite_templates() by @zdenop in #128
Ensures proper destruction of pixl. by @zdenop in #129
Prerelease maintenance  by @zdenop in #132
chore: update meson wraps by @mia-0 in #134
CI: Release workflow by @mia-0 in #133
Gihub action fixes by @zdenop in #135
Use file VERSION for setting version by @zdenop in #136

New Contributors

@mia-0 made their first contribution in #125

Full Changelog: 0.30...0.31

        0.30
      
        
          
              

          
      
    

    
      
    
        
          

        
  
  
    

  


        Changes

Reject odd input depths that pixThresholdToBinary doesn't support.
Fix build with Leptonica >=1.83
Replace vs2008 solution with CMake
Fix Error heap-use-after-free
Fix typos in code
Support python 3, drop support for python2
Rename pdf.py to jbig2topdf.py
Add a switch to set dpi for images w/o dpi information
Add support for standalone .jb2
Suppress a chatty informational message unless requested
Neat symbolic threshold 0.92
Fix Endian issue on ARM #63
Neat symbolic threshold 0.92
Make the weight parameter adjustable
Add global BW threshold on 8 bpp images
Replace obsolete macro `AC_PROG_LIBTOOL'

# jbig2

[](#jbig2)

[jbig2enc](https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc) — an encoder for the **JBIG2** image compression format (the `jbig2` command). A single self-contained binary, built natively for Linux, macOS, and Windows.

[![CI](https://github.com/unpins/jbig2/actions/workflows/jbig2.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/unpins/jbig2/actions) [![Linux](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/2bc17ee504ee70c9ef0491d31217750e7551c2fee763a10d9b355c7678db9dec/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f62616467652f4c696e75782d2545322539432539332d737563636573733f6c6f676f3d6c696e7578266c6f676f436f6c6f723d7768697465)](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/2bc17ee504ee70c9ef0491d31217750e7551c2fee763a10d9b355c7678db9dec/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f62616467652f4c696e75782d2545322539432539332d737563636573733f6c6f676f3d6c696e7578266c6f676f436f6c6f723d7768697465) [![macOS](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/b2f4ba7f856c5e4057f92c002d491fb950a9dc5cf9402d80504554bab98b3e84/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f62616467652f6d61634f532d2545322539432539332d737563636573733f6c6f676f3d6170706c65266c6f676f436f6c6f723d7768697465)](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/b2f4ba7f856c5e4057f92c002d491fb950a9dc5cf9402d80504554bab98b3e84/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f62616467652f6d61634f532d2545322539432539332d737563636573733f6c6f676f3d6170706c65266c6f676f436f6c6f723d7768697465) [![Windows](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/9225c5b2698a75483c85e1c68efbc5c60c9ce562ee6181f07330fc00ae5de177/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f62616467652f57696e646f77732d2545322539432539332d737563636573733f6c6f676f3d77696e646f7773266c6f676f436f6c6f723d7768697465)](https://camo.githubusercontent.com/9225c5b2698a75483c85e1c68efbc5c60c9ce562ee6181f07330fc00ae5de177/68747470733a2f2f696d672e736869656c64732e696f2f62616467652f57696e646f77732d2545322539432539332d737563636573733f6c6f676f3d77696e646f7773266c6f676f436f6c6f723d7768697465)

Part of the [unpins](https://unpins.org) catalog; install it with [`unpin`](https://github.com/unpins/unpin): `unpin install jbig2`.

## Usage

[](#usage)

Run the `jbig2` program with [unpin](https://github.com/unpins/unpin):

unpin jbig2 --version                 # version banner
unpin jbig2 input.png \> out.jb2       # encode to a JBIG2 stream
unpin jbig2 -s -p page1.png page2.png # symbol-coded, PDF-ready output

To install it onto your PATH:

unpin install jbig2

JBIG2 is the bilevel-image codec used inside PDF and fax workflows; `jbig2` reads the usual raster formats (PNG/TIFF/JPEG/…, via leptonica) and emits the compressed JBIG2 data.

## Scope: encoder only (no `jbig2topdf.py`)

[](#scope-encoder-only-no-jbig2topdfpy)

Upstream jbig2enc also ships `jbig2topdf.py`, a small Python helper that stitches the `-s -p` multipage output (`output.sym` + `output.NNNN`) into a PDF. This package ships **only the `jbig2` encoder** — bundling a whole CPython runtime just to run a ~150-line PDF byte-emitter would dwarf the binary it serves and defeats the single-self-contained-binary model.

If you need the PDF-muxing step, run the encoder here to produce the symbol / page streams and pass them through upstream's [`jbig2topdf.py`](https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc/blob/master/jbig2topdf.py) with any Python 3 you already have:

jbig2 -s -p -b output page1.png page2.png   # → output.sym, output.0000, …
python3 jbig2topdf.py output \> out.pdf

## Build locally

[](#build-locally)

nix build github:unpins/jbig2
./result/bin/jbig2 --version

Or run directly:

nix run github:unpins/jbig2 -- --version

The first invocation will offer to add the [unpins.cachix.org](https://unpins.cachix.org) substituter so most pulls come pre-built.

## Manual download

[](#manual-download)

The [Releases](https://github.com/unpins/jbig2/releases) page has standalone binaries for manual download.

## Build notes

[](#build-notes)

-   **Encoder only.** Upstream's `jbig2topdf.py` is dropped (see above), and with it the `python3` input — it was present only so the install could patch that script's shebang. The output is just `bin/jbig2`; the static library, headers, and the `nix-support` metadata that would otherwise pin store paths are all trimmed, so the binary and its whole closure carry no `/nix/store` reference.
    
-   **leptonica + the image-codec stack, statically.** `jbig2` links leptonica, which pulls giflib / libjpeg-turbo / libpng / libtiff / libwebp / openjpeg / zlib. jbig2enc's `configure` probes leptonica with a bare `-lleptonica`, which can't resolve against a static `libleptonica.a`'s transitive closure; we feed the full closure via `pkg-config --static --libs lept` so both the probe and the final link succeed.
    
-   **Windows (mingw) cross.** The mingw image stack needs four extra touches to link fully static: libtiff's auto-detected libjpeg 8/12-bit *dual mode* is turned off (its cmake probe is a false positive — the mingw libjpeg-turbo `.a` has no `jpeg12_*` symbols); leptonica is built library-only and with `-DOPJ_STATIC` (so its openjpeg calls don't expect a DLL import lib); `libsharpyuv` is added to the link (libwebp splits it out); and the libtool link uses `-all-static` so the single `jbig2.exe` ships with no companion DLLs.
    
-   **Portable on every target.** Linux/Windows binaries are fully static (`file` reports `statically linked`; the `.exe` imports only Windows system DLLs); macOS links only `/usr/lib/libSystem` and `/usr/lib/libc++` (`otool -L`).
    

## About

The jbig2enc JBIG2 encoder (jbig2) as a single self-contained binary — Linux, macOS, Windows

Done. Here's what was installed:

**`tessdata/`** — `eng.traineddata` (4 MB) copied from system `/usr/share/tesseract-ocr/5/tessdata/`. The pipeline now uses this project-local copy by default.

**`native/linux/tesseract`** — wrapper script that sets `TESSDATA_PREFIX` to `./tessdata/` then delegates to `/usr/bin/tesseract`. `settings.jsonc` now points `tesseract.path` to this wrapper.

**jbig2enc** — could not be built on this system (no `libleptonica-dev`, no build tools, no sudo). The pipeline falls back to CCITT G4 automatically, which passes all 8 integration tests. The `bootstrap.sh` script handles jbig2enc building for production.

**`settings.jsonc`** defaults updated:
- `tesseract.path` → `./native/linux/tesseract`
- `tessdata.dir` → `./tessdata`

**`.gitignore`** already ignores `native/`, `tessdata/`, `jdk/` — no changes needed.

**New `SettingsTest`** added (3 tests) verifying JSONC comment parsing and that all keys in `settings.jsonc` are correctly loaded.

{
  "filePath": "/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/src/main/java/com/trulyfreeocr/util/PlatformUtils.java",
  "oldString": "import java.io.File;\n\n/**\n * Cross-platform detection utilities.\n *\n * Provides consistent enum values for OS and CPU architecture,\n * and resolves the project JDK location via the TFOCR_JAVA_HOME\n * environment variable (falling back to the running JVM's java.home).\n *\n * OS detection is based on the os.name system property:\n *   \"win\" in any case   → Os.WINDOWS\n *   \"mac\" in any case   → Os.MAC\n *   anything else       → Os.LINUX\n *\n * Architecture detection is based on os.arch:\n *   \"aarch64\" or \"arm64\" → Arch.AARCH64\n *   anything else         → Arch.X64\n */\npublic class PlatformUtils {\n\n    public enum Os { WINDOWS, LINUX, MAC }\n\n    public enum Arch { X64, AARCH64 }\n\n    public static Os detectOs() {\n        String raw = System.getProperty(\"os.name\").toLowerCase();\n        if (raw.contains(\"win\")) return Os.WINDOWS;\n        if (raw.contains(\"mac\")) return Os.MAC;\n        return Os.LINUX;\n    }\n\n    public static Arch detectArch() {\n        String raw = System.getProperty(\"os.arch\").toLowerCase();\n        if (raw.contains(\"aarch64\") || raw.contains(\"arm64\")) return Arch.AARCH64;\n        return Arch.X64;\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Returns the project-specific JDK home.\n     *\n     * Checks TFOCR_JAVA_HOME first (set by run.sh/bootstrap.sh),\n     * then falls back to the JVM that is running this code.\n     */\n    public static String getProjectJavaHome() {\n        String env = System.getenv(\"TFOCR_JAVA_HOME\");\n        if (env != null && !env.isEmpty()) return env;\n        return System.getProperty(\"java.home\");\n    }\n}",
  "newString": "import java.io.File;\n\n/**\n * Cross-platform detection utilities.\n *\n * Provides consistent enum values for OS and CPU architecture,\n * and resolves the project JDK location by checking (in order):\n *   1. TFOCR_JAVA_HOME environment variable\n *   2. jdk/ directory in the project root\n *   3. The JVM that is running this code (java.home)\n *\n * OS detection is based on the os.name system property:\n *   \"win\" in any case   → Os.WINDOWS\n *   \"mac\" in any case   → Os.MAC\n *   anything else       → Os.LINUX\n *\n * Architecture detection is based on os.arch:\n *   \"aarch64\" or \"arm64\" → Arch.AARCH64\n *   anything else         → Arch.X64\n */\npublic class PlatformUtils {\n\n    public enum Os { WINDOWS, LINUX, MAC }\n\n    public enum Arch { X64, AARCH64 }\n\n    public static Os detectOs() {\n        String raw = System.getProperty(\"os.name\").toLowerCase();\n        if (raw.contains(\"win\")) return Os.WINDOWS;\n        if (raw.contains(\"mac\")) return Os.MAC;\n        return Os.LINUX;\n    }\n\n    public static Arch detectArch() {\n        String raw = System.getProperty(\"os.arch\").toLowerCase();\n        if (raw.contains(\"aarch64\") || raw.contains(\"arm64\")) return Arch.AARCH64;\n        return Arch.X64;\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Returns the project-specific JDK home.\n     *\n     * Checks:\n     *   1. TFOCR_JAVA_HOME env var (set by run.sh/bootstrap.sh)\n     *   2. jdk/ directory in the project root (project-local install)\n     *   3. The running JVM's java.home system property\n     */\n    public static String getProjectJavaHome() {\n        String env = System.getenv(\"TFOCR_JAVA_HOME\");\n        if (env != null && !env.isEmpty()) return env;\n        File projectJdk = new File(\"jdk\");\n        if (projectJdk.isDirectory() && new File(projectJdk, \"bin/java\").canExecute()) {\n            return projectJdk.getAbsolutePath();\n        }\n        return System.getProperty(\"java.home\");\n    }\n}"
}
```

TFOCR_JAVA_HOME environment variable (set by run.sh/bootstrap.sh)\n     *   2. jdk/ directory in the project root (project-local install)\n     *   3. The JVM that is running this code (java.home system property)\n     */\n    public static String getProjectJavaHome() {\n        String env = System.getenv(\"TFOCR_JAVA_HOME\");\n        if (env != null && !env.isEmpty()) return env;\n        File projectJdk = new File(\"jdk\");\n        if (projectJdk.isDirectory() && new File(projectJdk, \"bin/java\").canExecute()) {\n            return projectJdk.getAbsolutePath();\n        }\n        return System.getProperty(\"java.home\");\n    }"
}

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. On Linux, there are standard library paths that ld.so always searches (`/lib`, `/usr/lib`) and the standard libs (libc, libm, libpthread, etc.) are in those paths. The "special" deps that are Tesseract-specific are:
- libtesseract.so.5
- liblept.so.5

The rest (libarchive, libcurl, libpng, libjpeg, libtiff, etc.) are common system libs that almost every Linux has.

So the minimum viable "project-local Tesseract" would be:
1. Copy tesseract binary to `native/linux/tesseract` (renaming the real binary, wrapper script calls it)
2. Copy libtesseract.so.5 and liblept.so.5 to `native/linux/lib/`
3. Wrapper script sets `LD_LIBRARY_PATH=native/linux/lib/:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH` then execs the real binary

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

jbig2enc is now installed and working in the project-local native directory. Now I need to update the JBIG2Compressor to also set LD_LIBRARY_PATH for the jbig2enc binary, since it also needs `libjbig2enc.so.0`.

Actually, looking at the JBIG2Compressor code, it uses `SubprocessRunner.run()` which just runs the binary. The binary needs `libjbig2enc.so.0` in the library path. Since jbig2enc is a simple binary that only needs this one extra lib (plus Leptonica and standard libs which are in system paths), I have two options:

1. Set LD_LIBRARY_PATH in the SubprocessRunner/ProcessBuilder for jbig2enc calls
2. Use a wrapper script for jbig2enc (like we did for tesseract)
3. Use `patchelf` or `chrpath` to set the RPATH in the jbig2enc binary to `$ORIGIN/../lib`

The cleanest approach is a wrapper script. Let me replace the jbig2enc binary with a wrapper.

Actually, looking at the JBIG2Compressor more carefully, it already has a `findJbig2enc()` method that searches `native/{os}/jbig2enc`. When it finds the binary, it calls `SubprocessRunner.run(jbig2enc, "-p", "-s", tempPng.getAbsolutePath())`. The subprocess runs the binary. If the binary's dynamic library dependencies can't be found, it'll fail.

I need to update the findJbig2enc method or the subprocess runner to set LD_LIBRARY_PATH for the jbig2 process. Let me update the SubprocessRunner to allow passing environment variables, or better yet, update JBIG2Compressor to set the environment when running jbig2enc.

Actually, the simplest approach: create a wrapper script for jbig2enc that sets LD_LIBRARY_PATH. Rename the binary to `jbig2enc.bin` and have the wrapper call it.

</details>

### Tool: bash

**Input:**

```json
{
  "command": "# Rename jbig2enc binary and create wrapper that sets LD_LIBRARY_PATH\nmv /home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/native/linux/jbig2enc /home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/native/linux/jbig2enc.bin\ncat > /home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/native/linux/jbig2enc << 'SCRIPT'\n#!/bin/bash\n# Project-local jbig2enc wrapper.\n# Sets LD_LIBRARY_PATH to find the project-local shared libraries.\nSCRIPT_DIR=\"$(cd \"$(dirname \"$0\")\" && pwd)\"\nexport LD_LIBRARY_PATH=\"$SCRIPT_DIR/lib${LD_LIBRARY_PATH:+:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH}\"\nexec \"$SCRIPT_DIR/jbig2enc.bin\" \"$@\"\nSCRIPT\nchmod +x /home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/native/linux/jbig2enc\necho \"=== verification ===\"\n/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/native/linux/jbig2enc --version 2>&1",
  "description": "Create jbig2enc wrapper with LD_LIBRARY_PATH",
  "timeout": 5000,
  "workdir": "/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR"
}
```

```

```

|---|---|---|

All wrapper scripts set `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to `native/linux/lib/` so the project-local shared libraries are found instead of system ones. The `.gitignore` already covers `native/`, `jdk/`, and `tessdata/`. All integration tests pass using the project-local JDK (`JAVA_HOME=/.../jdk`).

<content>
95: 
96: ## Configuration
97: 
98: All pipeline parameters are configurable via `settings.jsonc` in the project root directory (JSON with `//` and `/* */` comments). CLI flags override file settings, which override hardcoded defaults.
99: 
100: | Setting | Default | Description |
101: |---|---|---|
102: | `native.dir` | `./native` | Native binaries directory (jbig2enc, etc.) |
103: | `tessdata.dir` | `./tessdata` | Tesseract language data directory |
104: | `tesseract.path` | `tesseract` | Tesseract executable path/name |
105: | `tesseract.language` | `eng` | Tesseract language model |
106: | `tesseract.psm` | `1` | Page segmentation mode |
107: | `rendering.dpi` | `300` | PDF rendering resolution |
108: | `segmenter.tileSize` | `64` | Background normalization tile size (px) |
109: | `segmenter.percentile` | `0.95` | Background level percentile per tile |
110: | `segmenter.inpaintRadius` | `3` | Inpainting search radius (px) |
111: | `jbig2enc.flags` | `-p -s` | Flags passed to jbig2enc |
112: | `pdf.font` | `HELVETICA` | Standard 14 font for OCR text |
113: | `pdf.minFontSize` | `1` | Minimum OCR text font size (pt) |
114: | `output.file` | `output.pdf` | Default output file path |
115: 
116: CLI flags that override corresponding settings: `--dpi`, `--language`, `--psm`, `--native-dir`, `--tessdata-dir`, `-o`/`--output`, `--settings` (path to a custom settings file).
117: 
118: To use a custom settings file path:
119: ```bash
120: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar --settings /path/to/custom.jsonc input.pdf
121: ```
122: 
123: ---
124: 
125: ## Building
126: 
127: ```bash
128: # Build the fat JAR
129: ./gradlew build
130: 
131: # Run all tests
132: ./gradlew test
133: ```
134: 
135: Requires **Java 21+** (JDK) and **Tesseract OCR** CLI (`tesseract` on PATH) with English language data installed.
136: 
137: ## Testing
138: 
139: Tests use generated sample PDFs in `tests/test-files/`:
140: - `blank.pdf` — single blank US Letter page
141: - `simple-text.pdf` — one page with lorem ipsum text
142: - `multi-page.pdf` — three-page document with distinct content per page
143: - `two-column.pdf` — two-column layout
144: - `with-annotations.pdf` — bookmarks, links, and comments
145: - `noisy-scan.pdf` — simulated scanner noise and skew
146: 
147: Regenerate test PDFs with:
148: ```bash
149: python tests/generate_test_pdfs.py
150: ```
151: 
152: ## Current Status
153: 
154: | Phase | Component | Status |
155: |-------|-----------|--------|
156: | 1 | Gradle scaffold + CLI | Done |
157: | 2 | PageExtractor | Done |
158: | 3 | OCREngine | Done |
159: | 4 | ImageSegmenter | Done |
160: | 5 | PDFAssembler (basic) | Done |
161: | 6 | JBIG2Compressor + SubprocessRunner | Done |
162: | 7 | PDFAssembler JBIG2 integration | Done |
163: | 8 | MetadataPreserver | Done |
164: | 9 | bootstrap scripts | Done |
165: | 10 | Integration tests | Done |
166: 
167: ## Dependencies
168: 
169: ### Build & Runtime (from Gradle)
170: 
171: | Dependency | Version | License | Project |
172: |---|---|---|---|
173: | PDFBox | 3.0.6 | Apache 2.0 | https://pdfbox.apache.org |
174: | picocli | 4.7.6 | Apache 2.0 | https://picocli.info |
175: | Shadow plugin | 8.1.1 | Apache 2.0 | https://github.com/johnrengelman/shadow |
176: 
177: ### Test (from Gradle)
178: 
179: | Dependency | Version | License | Project |
180: |---|---|---|---|
181: | JUnit Jupiter | 5.11.4 | EPL 2.0 | https://junit.org/junit5/ |
182: | JUnit Platform Launcher | 1.11.4 | EPL 2.0 | https://junit.org/junit5/ |
183: 
184: ### Native / Bundled
185: 
186: | Dependency | License | Project |
187: |---|---|---|
188: | OpenJDK 21 LTS | GPL 2.0 + Classpath Exception | https://adoptium.net |
189: | Gradle | Apache 2.0 | https://gradle.org |
190: | Tesseract OCR | Apache 2.0 | https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract |
191: | Leptonica | BSD 2-Clause | https://github.com/DanBloomberg/leptonica |
192: | jbig2enc | Apache 2.0 | https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc |
193: 
194: ## License
195: 
196: TrulyFreeOCR itself is made available under the Apache 2.0 License. All dependencies listed above use permissive open-source licenses compatible with commercial use. A full `NOTICE.md` with copyright attributions is included in the repository.

(End of file - total 196 lines)

## User

Now we need to test/evaluate how good the OCR works. Make a plan for evaluation.

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The user wants me to create a plan for evaluating how well the OCR works. Since I'm in plan/read-only mode, I should not make any changes, just research and design a plan.

Let me think about what "evaluate OCR quality" means:

1. **Accuracy metrics** - How well does the OCR text match the ground truth?
2. **End-to-end pipeline quality** - Does the final PDF look correct? Is the text searchable?
3. **Speed/performance** - How fast is each phase?
4. **Comparison** - How does it compare to other tools?

Let me think about what we can actually measure:

### OCR Accuracy Metrics
- **Word Error Rate (WER)** or **Character Error Rate (CER)** - Compare OCR output to ground truth
- **Precision/Recall** - Fraction of words correctly recognized
- **Confidence scores** - Tesseract provides per-word confidence

### Ground Truth
We need ground truth to compare against. Options:
1. The test PDFs were generated by `generate_test_pdfs.py` - we know exactly what text is in them! This makes evaluation very straightforward.
2. We can extract the text from simple-text.pdf, multi-page.pdf, two-column.pdf using the known content from the Python generator.

### What to measure
1. **OCR accuracy** - Compare Tesseract output against known text
2. **Foreground mask quality** - Binary segmentation accuracy
3. **Background cleaning** - Visual quality of inpainted background
4. **PDF size** - Compression ratio
5. **Searchability** - Can the output PDF be searched? Can text be extracted?
6. **Metadata preservation** - Are bookmarks, annotations, etc. preserved?
7. **Pipeline speed** - Time per phase
8. **Image processing quality** - DPI, tile size, percentile effects

Let me explore the test PDF generator to understand what ground truth text we have.

Actually, I shouldn't make any edits or run non-readonly tools. But `webfetch` and `read` and `grep` and `task` are read-only. Let me look at the test PDF generator to understand what ground truth is available.

Actually wait, let me also look at the existing tests to understand what assertions are already being made.

Let me design the evaluation plan:

1. **OCR Character/Word Accuracy** - For generated PDFs with known text, compare OCR output to ground truth
2. **Segmentation Quality** - For generated PDFs, evaluate foreground/background separation
3. **Searchability** - Verify text extraction from output PDF
4. **Compression** - Compare input/output file sizes
5. **Metadata Preservation** - Verify bookmarks, annotations survive
6. **Performance Benchmarking** - Time each pipeline stage
7. **Regression Testing** - Compare output across code changes

Let me present this as a plan to the user.

### Tool: read

**Input:**

```json
{
  "filePath": "/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/tests/generate_test_pdfs.py"
}
```

**Output:**

```
<path>/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/tests/generate_test_pdfs.py</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: #!/usr/bin/env python3
2: """Generate test PDFs for TrulyFreeOCR test-driven development.
3: 
4: Each PDF is image-based (non-searchable) to simulate scanned documents.
5: Creates files in tests/test-files/. Run from project root.
6: """
7: 
8: import io
9: import os
10: from PIL import Image, ImageDraw, ImageFont
11: import fitz  # PyMuPDF
12: 
13: OUT_DIR = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "test-files")
14: os.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)
15: 
16: FONT_DIR = "/usr/share/fonts/truetype/dejavu"
17: W, H = 612, 792  # US Letter at 72 DPI
18: MARGIN = 50
19: FONT_SZ = 14
20: 
21: def font(name="DejaVuSans.ttf", sz=FONT_SZ):
22:     return ImageFont.truetype(os.path.join(FONT_DIR, name), sz)
23: 
24: def new_page():
25:     img = Image.new("RGB", (W, H), "white")
26:     draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)
27:     return img, draw
28: 
29: def save_image_pdf(pages, path):
30:     doc = fitz.open()
31:     for img in pages:
32:         buf = io.BytesIO()
33:         img.save(buf, format="JPEG", quality=95)
34:         buf.seek(0)
35:         pix = fitz.Pixmap(buf.read())
36:         page = doc.new_page(width=W, height=H)
37:         page.insert_image(fitz.Rect(0, 0, W, H), pixmap=pix)
38:     doc.save(path)
39:     doc.close()
40: 
41: 
42: # ── 1. blank.pdf ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
43: def make_blank():
44:     img, _ = new_page()
45:     save_image_pdf([img], os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "blank.pdf"))
46:     print("  blank.pdf")
47: 
48: # ── 2. simple-text.pdf ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
49: def make_simple_text():
50:     img, d = new_page()
51:     fnt = font()
52:     lines = [
53:         "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.",
54:         "Pack my box with five dozen liquor jugs.",
55:         "Sphinx of black quartz, judge my vow.",
56:         "",
57:         "This is a test document for OCR validation.",
58:         "It contains English text rendered as an image,",
59:         "simulating a scanned page that is not searchable.",
60:         "",
61:         "The pipeline should extract this text via Tesseract,",
62:         "then embed it as invisible text over the image.",
63:         "The output PDF should be searchable while retaining",
64:         "the original visual appearance.",
65:     ]
66:     y = MARGIN
67:     for line in lines:
68:         d.text((MARGIN, y), line, fill="black", font=fnt)
69:         y += 22
70:     save_image_pdf([img], os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "simple-text.pdf"))
71:     print("  simple-text.pdf")
72: 
73: # ── 3. multi-page.pdf ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
74: def make_multi_page():
75:     pages = []
76:     page_data = [
77:         ["Page 1: Introduction",
78:          "This is the first page of a multi-page document.",
79:          "It contains introductory text about the topic.",
80:          "Each page has different content to test that",
81:          "the OCR pipeline processes all pages correctly."],
82:         ["Page 2: Methodology",
83:          "The approach uses a 6-step pipeline:",
84:          "1. Page extraction via PDFBox at 300 DPI",
85:          "2. Image segmentation via Leptonica (lept4j)",
86:          "3. JBIG2 compression of text mask",
87:          "4. OCR via Tesseract (Tess4J)",
88:          "5. PDF re-assembly with searchable text",
89:          "6. Metadata preservation (bookmarks, annotations)"],
90:         ["Page 3: Results",
91:          "The output is a searchable, highly-compressed PDF",
92:          "that maintains the original visual appearance",
93:          "while adding a hidden text layer for searching.",
94:          "All metadata from the source PDF is preserved."],
95:     ]
96:     for title, *body in page_data:
97:         img, d = new_page()
98:         d.text((MARGIN, MARGIN), title, fill="black",
99:                font=font("DejaVuSans.ttf", 18))
100:         y = MARGIN + 40
101:         for line in body:
102:             d.text((MARGIN, y), line, fill="black", font=font())
103:             y += 22
104:         pages.append(img)
105:     save_image_pdf(pages, os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "multi-page.pdf"))
106:     print("  multi-page.pdf")
107: 
108: # ── 4. two-column.pdf ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
109: def make_two_column():
110:     img, d = new_page()
111:     col_w = (W - 3 * MARGIN) // 2
112:     x1, x2 = MARGIN, MARGIN * 2 + col_w
113:     fnt = font()
114:     body = (
115:         "Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing "
116:         "elit. Sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et "
117:         "dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis "
118:         "nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip "
119:         "ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in "
120:         "reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore "
121:         "eu fugiat nulla pariatur. Excepteur sint occaecat "
122:         "cupidatat non proident, sunt in culpa qui officia "
123:         "deserunt mollit anim id est laborum."
124:     )
125:     # Wrap into ~40-char lines
126:     words = body.split()
127:     lines = []
128:     for i in range(0, len(words), 8):
129:         lines.append(" ".join(words[i:i+8]))
130: 
131:     # Left column
132:     d.text((x1, MARGIN), "Left Column", fill="black",
133:            font=font("DejaVuSans.ttf", 16))
134:     y = MARGIN + 30
135:     for line in lines:
136:         d.text((x1, y), line, fill="black", font=fnt)
137:         y += 18
138: 
139:     # Right column
140:     d.text((x2, MARGIN), "Right Column", fill="black",
141:            font=font("DejaVuSans.ttf", 16))
142:     y = MARGIN + 30
143:     for line in lines:
144:         d.text((x2, y), line, fill="black", font=fnt)
145:         y += 18
146: 
147:     save_image_pdf([img], os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "two-column.pdf"))
148:     print("  two-column.pdf")
149: 
150: # ── 5. with-annotations.pdf ─────────────────────────────────────────────
151: def make_with_annotations():
152:     pages = []
153:     # Create multi-page document with bookmarks
154:     titles = ["Chapter 1: Getting Started",
155:               "Chapter 2: Configuration",
156:               "Chapter 3: Usage"]
157:     for i, title in enumerate(titles, 1):
158:         img, d = new_page()
159:         d.text((MARGIN, MARGIN), title, fill="black",
160:                font=font("DejaVuSans.ttf", 18))
161:         d.text((MARGIN, MARGIN + 40),
162:                f"This is page {i} of the annotated document.",
163:                fill="black", font=font())
164:         d.text((MARGIN, MARGIN + 70),
165:                "This PDF includes bookmarks and annotations.",
166:                fill="black", font=font())
167:         pages.append(img)
168: 
169:     doc = fitz.open()
170:     for i, img in enumerate(pages):
171:         buf = io.BytesIO()
172:         img.save(buf, format="JPEG", quality=95)
173:         buf.seek(0)
174:         pix = fitz.Pixmap(buf.read())
175:         page = doc.new_page(width=W, height=H)
176:         page.insert_image(fitz.Rect(0, 0, W, H), pixmap=pix)
177: 
178:         # Add text annotation on first page
179:         if i == 0:
180:             annot = page.add_text_annot(
181:                 (100, 100), "This is a sticky note annotation."
182:             )
183: 
184:         # Add underline annotation on first page
185:         if i == 0:
186:             page.add_underline_annot(
187:                 fitz.Rect(MARGIN, MARGIN, MARGIN + 200, MARGIN + 20)
188:             )
189: 
190:     # Add bookmarks (outline)
191:     toc = [
192:         [1, "Chapter 1", 1],
193:         [1, "Chapter 2", 2],
194:         [1, "Chapter 3", 3],
195:     ]
196:     doc.set_toc(toc)
197: 
198:     doc.save(os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "with-annotations.pdf"))
199:     doc.close()
200:     print("  with-annotations.pdf")
201: 
202: # ── 6. noisy-scan.pdf ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
203: def make_noisy_scan():
204:     import random
205:     random.seed(42)
206:     img, d = new_page()
207:     fnt = font()
208: 
209:     lines = [
210:         "This page simulates a noisy scanned document.",
211:         "It includes speckles, uneven lighting, and",
212:         "slight rotation to test image preprocessing.",
213:     ]
214:     y = MARGIN
215:     for line in lines:
216:         d.text((MARGIN, y), line, fill="black", font=fnt)
217:         y += 22
218: 
219:     # Add noise
220:     pixels = img.load()
221:     for _ in range(3000):
222:         x = random.randint(0, W - 1)
223:         y = random.randint(0, H - 1)
224:         v = random.randint(0, 60)
225:         pixels[x, y] = (v, v, v)
226: 
227:     # Add a gradient (simulating uneven lighting)
228:     for y in range(H):
229:         shade = int(30 * (y / H))
230:         for x in range(W):
231:             r, g, b = pixels[x, y]
232:             pixels[x, y] = (
233:                 max(0, r - shade),
234:                 max(0, g - shade),
235:                 max(0, b - shade),
236:             )
237: 
238:     save_image_pdf([img], os.path.join(OUT_DIR, "noisy-scan.pdf"))
239:     print("  noisy-scan.pdf")
240: 
241: 
242: if __name__ == "__main__":
243:     print("Generating test PDFs...")
244:     make_blank()
245:     make_simple_text()
246:     make_multi_page()
247:     make_two_column()
248:     make_with_annotations()
249:     make_noisy_scan()
250:     print(f"\nDone. Files in {OUT_DIR}/")

(End of file - total 250 lines)
</content>
```

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now I have a good understanding of the existing test infrastructure. Let me design the evaluation plan.

Key points:
1. The test PDFs have known ground truth text (defined in generate_test_pdfs.py)
2. Existing tests do basic sanity checks (non-null, has words, confidence > 0)
3. No quantitative OCR accuracy metrics exist yet
4. No performance benchmarks exist

Plan structure:

## Evaluation Plan

### 1. OCR Accuracy
- **Metric**: Character Error Rate (CER) and Word Error Rate (WER)
- **Method**: Run OCR on simple-text.pdf, multi-page.pdf, two-column.pdf, compare output to ground truth from generate_test_pdfs.py
- **Ground truth sources**: The literal text strings in generate_test_pdfs.py for each PDF
- **Acceptance criteria**: WER < 5% for clean text, < 15% for noisy scan

### 2. Segmentation Quality
- **Metric**: Foreground pixel coverage, background uniformity
- **Method**: For generated PDFs, the foreground mask should capture all text pixels. Measure Jaccard index or similar if we can render ground truth masks
- **Acceptance criteria**: Foreground mask captures > 90% of text pixels, < 5% false positives on blank areas

### 3. Searchability Verification
- **Metric**: Can text be extracted from the output PDF via PDFBox's text extraction?
- **Method**: Run full pipeline, extract text from output PDF, compare to ground truth
- **Acceptance criteria**: Output text contains all key content words

### 4. Compression Ratio
- **Metric**: Output size / input size
- **Method**: Compare sizes across different input PDFs
- **Acceptance criteria**: Output is smaller than input for multi-page docs, comparable for single page

### 5. Metadata Preservation
- **Metric**: Bookmark count, annotation count match
- **Method**: Extract from output PDF, compare to with-annotations.pdf
- **Acceptance criteria**: All bookmarks and annotations preserved

### 6. Performance Benchmarking
- **Metric**: Time per pipeline stage (extraction, segmentation, OCR, assembly)
- **Method**: Instrument PipelineIntegrationTest with timestamps
- **Acceptance criteria**: Baseline timing for regression detection

### 7. Parameter Sensitivity
- **Metric**: How DPI, tile size, percentile, inpaint radius affect accuracy
- **Method**: Run OCR with different settings, measure WER
- **Goal**: Find optimal default parameters

---

Actually, I should also ask the user what their specific concerns or priorities are. But they asked for a plan, so let me present a thorough plan and ask if they want to adjust scope.

Let me also check the existing integration tests to understand what's already covered.

</details>

137:             String text = stripper.getText(output);
138:             assertTrue(text.toLowerCase().contains("quick"));
139:         }
140:     }
141: 
142:     @Test
143:     void fullPipeline_outputFileSizeIsReasonable() throws IOException {
144:         File input = new File("tests/test-files/simple-text.pdf");
145:         var pages = extractor.extractPages(input);
146:         var segmented = pages.stream().map(segmenter::segment).toList();
147:         var backgrounds = segmented.stream().map(SegmentedImage::getCleanedBackground).toList();
148:         var foregroundMasks = segmented.stream().map(SegmentedImage::getForegroundMask).toList();
149:         var ocrResults = processOcr(pages);
150: 
151:         File tempOutput = File.createTempFile("tfocr-integration-", ".pdf");
152:         tempOutput.deleteOnExit();
153:         try (PDDocument output = assembler.assemble(input, backgrounds, foregroundMasks, ocrResults)) {
154:             output.save(tempOutput);
155:         }
156:         // Output should be reasonably sized (not trivially tiny, not enormous)
157:         long size = tempOutput.length();
158:         assertTrue(size > 1000, "Output PDF should be at least 1KB, was " + size + " bytes");
159:         // At 300 DPI for US Letter, even compressed, expect > 10KB
160:         assertTrue(size < 100_000_000, "Output PDF should be under 100MB, was " + size + " bytes");
161:     }
162: 
163:     // ── Helpers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
164: 
165:     @FunctionalInterface
166:     private interface OutputValidator {
167:         void validate(PDDocument output, File sourceFile) throws IOException;
168:     }
169: 
170:     private void runPipeline(String testPdfName, OutputValidator validator) throws IOException {
171:         File input = new File("tests/test-files/" + testPdfName);
172:         assertTrue(input.exists(), "Test PDF not found: " + input);
173: 
174:         var pages = extractor.extractPages(input);
175:         assertNotNull(pages);
176:         assertFalse(pages.isEmpty());
177: 
178:         var segmented = pages.stream().map(segmenter::segment).toList();
179:         var backgrounds = segmented.stream().map(SegmentedImage::getCleanedBackground).toList();
180:         var ocrResults = processOcr(pages);
181: 
182:         try (PDDocument output = assembler.assemble(input, backgrounds, ocrResults)) {
183:             assertNotNull(output);
184:             validator.validate(output, input);
185:         }
186:     }
187: 
188:     private List<PageResult> processOcr(List<BufferedImage> pages) {
189:         return pages.stream().map(p -> {
190:             try { return engine.ocr(p); }
191:             catch (IOException e) { throw new RuntimeException(e); }
192:         }).toList();
193:     }
194: }

60:     @Test

63:         SegmentedImage result = segmenter.segment(pages.get(0));
64:         BufferedImage mask = result.getForegroundMask();

71:                     blackPixels++;
72:                 }
73:             }
74:         }

85:         BufferedImage mask = result.getForegroundMask();

88:         int blackPixels = 0;
89:         for (int y = 0; y < height; y++) {
90:             for (int x = 0; x < width; x++) {

92:                     blackPixels++;
93:                 }
94:             }
95:         }

98:                 "Text page should have >0.1% foreground pixels, got " +
99:                 (100.0 * blackPixels / total) + "%");
100:     }
101: 
102:     @Test
103:     void segment_cleanedBackground_reducesTextNoise() throws IOException {
104:         var pages = extractor.extractPages(new File("tests/test-files/simple-text.pdf"));
105:         BufferedImage page = pages.get(0);
106:         SegmentedImage result = segmenter.segment(page);
107:         BufferedImage cleaned = result.getCleanedBackground();
108: 
109:         // Compare cleaned vs original: text areas should be filled in
110:         BufferedImage mask = result.getForegroundMask();
111:         int width = page.getWidth();
112:         int height = page.getHeight();
113:         int textPixels = 0;
114:         int differentPixels = 0;
115:         for (int y = 0; y < height; y++) {
116:             for (int x = 0; x < width; x++) {
117:                 if ((mask.getRGB(x, y) & 0xFFFFFF) == 0) {
118:                     textPixels++;
119:                     int origRgb = page.getRGB(x, y);
120:                     int cleanRgb = cleaned.getRGB(x, y);
121:                     if (origRgb != cleanRgb) {
122:                         differentPixels++;
123:                     }
124:                 }
125:             }
126:         }
127:         // Most foreground pixels should differ (text filled in)
128:         assertTrue(textPixels > 0);
129:         assertTrue((double) differentPixels / textPixels > 0.5,
130:                 "Most foreground pixels should differ between original and cleaned");
131:     }
132: }

(End of file - total 132 lines)
</content>
```

- Evaluate inpainted background quality (pixel difference ratios)

- Tile size: 32, 64, 128

- Time per stage
- Memory usage

- Markdown or CSV output

Here's my proposed evaluation plan:

## OCR Evaluation Plan

### 1. Ground Truth Corpus

The test PDFs in `tests/generate_test_pdfs.py` contain known text. I'll extract ground truth strings into a Java constants class so tests can reference them programmatically.

| Test PDF | Total Words | Source |
|---|---|---|
| `simple-text.pdf` | ~90 | Lines in `make_simple_text()` |
| `multi-page.pdf` | ~100 across 3 pages | Lines in `make_multi_page()` |
| `two-column.pdf` | ~60 | Lorum ipsum in `make_two_column()` |
| `noisy-scan.pdf` | ~40 + speckle/gradient | Lines in `make_noisy_scan()` |

### 2. OCR Accuracy Metrics

| Metric | How | Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **Word Error Rate (WER)** | Levenshtein distance at word level vs ground truth | < 5% clean, < 15% noisy |
| **Character Error Rate (CER)** | Levenshtein distance at char level | < 2% clean, < 10% noisy |
| **Word Recall** | Fraction of ground-truth words found in output | > 95% |
| **Word Precision** | Fraction of output words that appear in ground truth | > 90% |
| **Mean Confidence** | Average Tesseract confidence score | > 80 |
| **Bounding Box Overlap** | IoU between OCR bboxes and rendered text regions | qualitative |

### 3. Segmentation Quality

| Metric | How |
|---|---|
| **Blank page false positives** | Foreground pixel % on blank.pdf (already tested: < 5%) |
| **Text page recall** | Are all rendered text pixels captured in the mask? (Check pixel overlap) |
| **Inpaint quality** | % of foreground pixels that differ between original and cleaned background (already tested: > 50%) |

### 4. End-to-End Pipeline

| Test | Verification |
|---|---|
| **Searchability** | Extract text from output PDF, compare to ground truth per page |
| **Compression ratio** | Output / input file size, with and without jbig2enc |
| **Metadata round-trip** | Bookmark count, annotation count, document info fields |
| **CCITT G4 fallback** | Output size and quality when jbig2enc is missing |

### 5. Parameter Sensitivity

Run OCR + segmentation on `simple-text.pdf` varying one parameter at a time:

| Parameter | Values to Test |
|---|---|
| `rendering.dpi` | 150, 200, **300**, 400, 600 |
| `segmenter.tileSize` | 16, 32, **64**, 128, 256 |
| `segmenter.percentile` | 0.85, 0.90, **0.95**, 0.98 |
| `segmenter.inpaintRadius` | 1, **3**, 5, 7 |

Bold = current default. Measure WER and execution time for each.

### 6. Performance Baseline

| Stage | What to Time |
|---|---|
| PageExtractor | Render all pages at 300 DPI |
| ImageSegmenter | Grayscale → normalize → Otsu → inpaint |
| OCREngine | Tesseract subprocess (TSV) |
| PDFAssembler | Background + mask + text layers |
| **Total** | Full pipeline wall-clock |

### 7. Deliverables

- **`src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/OcrAccuracyTest.java`** — WER/CER/precision/recall for each test PDF
- **`src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/SegmentationEvalTest.java`** — foreground pixel coverage, inpaint quality
- **`src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/ParameterSweepTest.java`** — DPI / tile / percentile / radius sweep with WER results
- **`src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/PerformanceBenchmark.java`** — per-stage timing
- **`EVALUATION.md`** — summary of results, recommendations for default parameters

Wanted to get your feedback before implementing — is this scope right, or would you like me to focus on specific areas (e.g., just OCR accuracy + the parameter sweep)?

## User

Your ground truth corpus is too naive, too toy. It should be looking at pdf containing hundreds of pages, tens of thousands pages.

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The user is giving me feedback on my evaluation plan. They're saying the ground truth corpus I proposed (simple-text.pdf with ~90 words, multi-page.pdf with ~100 words, etc.) is too naive and toy. They want evaluation against PDFs with hundreds of pages, tens of thousands of pages - i.e., realistic, large-scale documents.

Let me think about what this means and update the plan accordingly. I'm in plan mode, so I should NOT make any edits. I should discuss and plan with the user.

Key points the user is making:
1. The test PDFs I proposed are too small (90-100 words) - not realistic
2. Real-world documents have hundreds to tens of thousands of pages
3. We need to evaluate at scale

This changes the evaluation plan significantly:

1. **Generate large-scale test PDFs**: We need to generate PDFs with hundreds/thousands of pages with known ground truth
2. **Performance at scale**: How does the pipeline handle large documents?
3. **Memory usage**: Does it OOM on 10,000 page documents?
4. **Compression at scale**: JBIG2's global symbol dictionary becomes more effective with more pages
5. **Parameter tuning for scale**: Different parameters might work better for large documents

Let me think about what a realistic evaluation corpus would look like:

### Large-scale Corpus Generation
- We already have `generate_test_pdfs.py` with PyMuPDF - we can extend it to generate hundreds/thousands of pages
- For ground truth, we can use a language model or a text corpus (e.g., Project Gutenberg books) to generate realistic page content
- Each page would have known text that we can compare against

### Evaluation Dimensions at Scale

**Performance:**
- Page extraction time (scales linearly with page count)
- OCR time (per-page, does it slow down with more pages?)
- Segmentation time
- PDF assembly time (especially with large documents)
- Memory heap usage per stage
- Garbage collection pressure

**JBIG2 at scale:**
- JBIG2's global symbol dictionary should give better compression with more pages
- How does the symbol table size grow with page count?
- When does the CCITT G4 fallback become more attractive?

**Bottleneck identification:**
- Where does the pipeline spend most of its time at scale?
- Is OCR the bottleneck (it usually is)?
- Is there any place where we have O(n²) behavior?

### Practical Considerations
- We can't generate truly massive PDFs in test time constraints
- But we should test with at least 100, 500, and 1000+ page documents
- Use a text corpus (e.g., lorem ipsum generator or a public domain book) as source text
- Generate PDFs programmatically with known content

Let me ask the user about their specific concerns and then revise the plan.

</details>

You're right. The existing test PDFs are toy documents that won't reveal scaling issues. A real evaluation needs:

**Corpus size**: Hundreds to thousands of pages with known ground truth. We can extend `generate_test_pdfs.py` to produce configurable-size PDFs using a public-domain text corpus (e.g., Project Gutenberg) or a procedural text generator, so each page has known content we can compute WER/CER against.

**Evaluation dimensions that only appear at scale:**

| Concern | Why it matters at scale |
|---|---|
| **Page extraction throughput** | 10,000 pages × 300 DPI = ~200 GB of pixel data — does it OOM? |
| **OCR batch processing** | Tesseract CLI on thousands of pages — subprocess overhead per page adds up |
| **Temp file accumulation** | OCR drops temp PNGs everywhere — does temp cleanup keep up? |
| **JBIG2 symbol table growth** | Global dictionary across 10K pages — does `-p -s` scale or blow up? |
| **PDF assembly time** | Building a 10K-page PDF with per-page content streams — memory vs streaming tradeoff |
| **Metadata round-trip** | Thousands of bookmarks/annotations — does the COS deep copy get slow? |

**Questions for you to narrow scope:**

1. **Target corpus size** — What order of magnitude? 100, 1,000, 10,000 pages?
2. **Text source** — Use a known corpus (e.g., a Gutenberg book → one paragraph per page) or procedurally generate sentences with known word counts? The former gives realistic language, the latter gives exact ground truth.
3. **Priority** — Is the main concern *OCR accuracy at scale* (does WER degrade with page count?), *pipeline throughput* (how fast does it process N pages?), or *memory stability* (does it handle 10K pages without crashing)?

1. assume a typical pdf file contains mostly text, has pages range from 10 to 500, like scanned legal documents, business reports, or research papers.

2. for now let's focus on OCR accuracy.

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The user wants me to refine the evaluation plan based on their feedback:

1. Target corpus: 10-500 pages, typical of scanned legal docs, business reports, research papers
2. Focus: OCR accuracy

They also indicated I'm in plan/read-only mode, so I should NOT make any edits. I should just present the refined plan.

Let me think about what a realistic evaluation looks like for 10-500 page scanned documents:

### Corpus Design

For OCR accuracy evaluation, we need ground truth text. Options:
1. **Use real scanned PDFs with known text** - hard to find with ground truth
2. **Generate synthetic scanned PDFs** - like we do now, but with realistic content
3. **Use a public dataset** - like UW-III, ICDAR datasets, but those are usually images, not PDFs
4. **Render real text PDFs to images** - Take a real text PDF (like a Gutenberg book), render it as non-searchable image PDF, then OCR it and compare to the original text

Option 4 is the most realistic and scalable approach:
- Download a public domain book from Project Gutenberg as text
- Render it as a PDF using a tool (we already have PyMuPDF)
- Convert to image-only PDF (simulating a scan)
- Run the pipeline
- Compare OCR output to the original text

This gives us:
- Realistic language (not toy sentences)
- Variable page lengths
- Known ground truth (the original text)
- Scalable to any number of pages

For 10-500 pages, a book like "A Tale of Two Cities" (~400 pages) or a collection of research papers would work.

### Specific Plan

1. **Generate realistic ground truth PDF sets:**
   - 10-page sample (short report)
   - 50-page sample (legal document)
   - 200-page sample (business report)
   - 500-page sample (research thesis/book)
   - Each generated by rendering text from a real source as image-only PDF

2. **Text sources:**
   - Project Gutenberg public domain books
   - We can extract text from any source and pipe it through our PDF generator

3. **Accuracy measurement across page counts:**
   - Does WER stay constant across pages, or does it drift?
   - Are certain types of content (tables, headings, mixed fonts) systematically worse?

4. **What to measure per page:**
   - WER, CER, precision, recall
   - Tesseract confidence distribution
   - Processing time per page

Let me present this refined plan to the user.

</details>

Makes sense. Here's the refined plan focused on realistic OCR accuracy evaluation:

---

## Corpus Design

**Source**: Public-domain text (Project Gutenberg) rendered as image-only PDFs — realistic language, known ground truth, and we already have PyMuPDF to generate them.

**Sizes to match your document profiles**:

| Profile | Pages | Text Source | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short report | 10 | Single article/chapter | ~5,000 words |
| Legal document | 50 | Extended excerpt | ~25,000 words |
| Business report | 200 | Full book | ~100,000 words |
| Research thesis | 500 | Long book or multi-doc concatenation | ~250,000 words |

Each PDF is generated by rendering the source text onto US Letter pages (like the existing test PDF generator does), producing a non-searchable image PDF that simulates a scanned document.

## What to Measure

**Per page:**
- Character Error Rate (CER) and Word Error Rate (WER)
- Word recall / precision
- Mean Tesseract confidence
- Processing time

**Across the corpus:**
- WER distribution (p10, p50, p90) — does accuracy degrade with page count?
- Outlier pages — which pages have the worst WER, and what's on them?
- Page-to-page consistency (confidence stability, bbox size trends)

## Implementation Sketch

1. **Ground-truth extraction script** — reads the source text, splits into pages per our layout (MARGIN, FONT_SZ, line spacing), stores `(page_num, expected_text)` in a JSON file alongside the generated PDF.

2. **`OcrAccuracyTest.java`** — loads the JSON ground truth, runs the pipeline, computes WER/CER per page using Levenshtein distance, reports aggregate statistics.

3. **Noise realism option** — optionally apply the same speckle + gradient we use in `noisy-scan.pdf` to test robustness on non-ideal scans.

## Open Questions

1. **Corpus source** — Should I hardcode a specific Gutenberg text (e.g., a short novel like *A Christmas Carol* ~80 pages, or a longer one like *Pride and Prejudice* ~200 pages), or write a tool that accepts any `.txt` file so you can supply your own corpus?

2. **Noise levels** — Evaluate on clean image PDFs only, or also on noisy variants (speckle, gradient, low contrast) to stress-test the ImageSegmenter?

3. **Scoring granularity** — Report aggregate WER across the whole document, or also flag individual pages where WER exceeds a threshold?

Let me think about the implementation plan:

### Phase 1: Python corpus generator (`tests/generate_eval_corpus.py`)

```python
# Takes a Gutenberg text URL (default: Moby Dick)
# Splits text into pages with known coordinates
# Generates:
#   - eval/moby-dick-10.pdf  (first 10 pages)
#   - eval/moby-dick-50.pdf  (first 50 pages)
#   - eval/moby-dick-200.pdf (first 200 pages)
#   - eval/moby-dick-500.pdf (first 500 pages)
#   - eval/moby-dick-ground-truth.json  (page_num → expected_text)
```

The script:
1. Downloads the text from Gutenberg (plain text UTF-8)
2. Strips Gutenberg header/footer boilerplate
3. Fits text onto pages using same layout as existing generator (MARGIN, FONT_SZ, line spacing)
4. Renders each page as an image-only PDF (same JPEG-in-PDF approach as existing generator)
5. Saves ground-truth JSON with exact text per page

### Phase 2: Ground-truth JSON format

```json
{
  "source": "https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2701.txt.utf-8",
  "title": "Moby Dick",
  "author": "Herman Melville",
  "layout": {"fontSize": 14, "lineHeight": 22, "margin": 50, "width": 612, "height": 792},
  "pages": [
    {"page": 1, "text": "Call me Ishmael..."},
    {"page": 2, "text": "..."}
  ]
}
```

### Phase 3: Java evaluation test

New file: `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/OcrAccuracyTest.java`

```java
// Loads ground-truth JSON
// For each eval PDF (10, 50, 200, 500 pages):
//   - Run PageExtractor → OCREngine for each page

//   - Flag pages where WER > threshold (e.g., 5% for clean)

## Files to Create

### 1. `tests/generate_eval_corpus.py`

New Python script that:

- Downloads **Moby Dick** (Gutenberg #2701, ~210K words, public domain) plain text UTF-8
- Strips Gutenberg header/footer boilerplate
- Fits text onto US Letter pages using the same layout as `generate_test_pdfs.py` (`MARGIN=50`, `FONT_SZ=14`, `line_height=22`)
- Generates four PDFs in `tests/eval-corpus/`:
  - `moby-dick-010p.pdf` (first 10 pages)
  - `moby-dick-050p.pdf` (first 50 pages)
  - `moby-dick-200p.pdf` (first 200 pages)
  - `moby-dick-500p.pdf` (first 500 pages)
- Writes ground-truth JSON alongside each PDF:

```json
{
  "source": "https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2701",
  "title": "Moby Dick",
  "pages": [
    {"page": 1, "words": ["Call", "me", "Ishmael", ...]},
    {"page": 2, ...}
  ]
}
```

`tests/eval-corpus/` added to `.gitignore`.

### 2. `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/OcrAccuracyTest.java`

- Loads the ground-truth JSON
- For each eval PDF (10, 50, 200, 500 pages):
  - Runs `PageExtractor.extractPages()` → `OCREngine.ocr()` per page
  - Per-page metrics: WER, CER, word recall, word precision, mean confidence, processing time
  - Aggregate metrics across the whole document
- Prints a tabular report: per-page WER (flagged if > 5%), document summary
- Uses `@Tag("eval")` — excluded from `./gradlew test`, run explicitly

### 3. Levenshtein distance

A small static helper in the test class (no new dep needed).

### 4. Update `Evaluation.md`

Replace the placeholder plan with the refined methodology above.

### Tool: write

**Input:**

```json
{
  "filePath": "/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/tests/generate_eval_corpus.py",
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"Generate realistic evaluation corpus for OCR accuracy testing.\n\nDownloads public-domain text from Project Gutenberg, renders it as\nimage-only PDFs at various page counts, and writes ground-truth JSON\nfor automated WER/CER evaluation.\n\nGenerated files (saved in tests/eval-corpus/):\n  moby-dick-010p.pdf  + moby-dick-010p.json  (10 pages)\n  moby-dick-050p.pdf  + moby-dick-050p.json  (50 pages)\n  moby-dick-200p.pdf  + moby-dick-200p.json  (200 pages)\n  moby-dick-500p.pdf  + moby-dick-500p.json  (500 pages)\n\nRun once to generate the corpus, then reuse across eval runs.\nPass --force to regenerate from scratch.\n\"\"\"\n\nimport argparse\nimport gzip\nimport io\nimport json\nimport os\nimport re\nimport sys\nimport urllib.request\n\nfrom PIL import Image, ImageDraw, ImageFont\nimport fitz\n\nOUT_DIR = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), \"eval-corpus\")\nos.makedirs(OUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)\n\n# Same layout constants as tests/generate_test_pdfs.py\nFONT_DIR = \"/usr/share/fonts/truetype/dejavu\"\nW, H = 612, 792  # US Letter at 72 DPI\nMARGIN = 50\nFONT_SZ = 14\nLINE_HEIGHT = 22\nMAX_WIDTH = W - 2 * MARGIN  # pixels available for text per line\n\nGUTENBERG_URL = \"https://www.gutenberg.org/files/2701/2701-0.txt\"\n\nPAGE_COUNTS = [10, 50, 200, 500]\n\n\ndef download_text(url):\n    print(f\"  Downloading {url}...\")\n    with urllib.request.urlopen(url) as resp:\n        data = resp.read()\n    # Gutenberg serves UTF-8 or Windows-1252; try UTF-8 first.\n    try:\n        return data.decode(\"utf-8\")\n    except UnicodeDecodeError:\n        return data.decode(\"windows-1252\")\n\n\ndef strip_gutenberg_boilerplate(text):\n    # Remove header: keep everything after \"START OF THIS PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK\"\n    m = re.search(r\"\\*\\*\\* START OF (THIS PROJECT|THE PROJECT) GUTENBERG EBOOK.*?\\*\\*\\*\", text)\n    if m:\n        text = text[m.end():]\n    # Remove footer: stop before \"END OF THIS PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK\"\n    m = re.search(r\"\\*\\*\\* END OF (THIS PROJECT|THE PROJECT) GUTENBERG EBOOK.*?\\*\\*\\*\", text)\n    if m:\n        text = text[:m.start()]\n    return text.strip()\n\n\ndef clean_line(line):\n    # Keep only printable ASCII-ish text; drop Gutenberg illustration markers etc.\n    line = line.strip()\n    if not line:\n        return \"\"\n    # Skip lines that are mostly punctuation or illustration markers\n    if re.search(r\"[_=]{4,}\", line):\n        return \"\"\n    if re.search(r\"^\\[Illustration\", line):\n        return \"\"\n    return line\n\n\ndef get_font(size=FONT_SZ):\n    return ImageFont.truetype(os.path.join(FONT_DIR, \"DejaVuSans.ttf\"), size)\n\n\ndef wrap_line(draw, text, font, max_width):\n    \"\"\"Word-wrap text to fit within max_width pixels using the given font.\"\"\"\n    words = text.split()\n    if not words:\n        return [\"\"]\n    lines = []\n    current = words[0]\n    for w in words[1:]:\n        candidate = current + \" \" + w\n        w_px = draw.textlength(candidate, font=font)\n        if w_px <= max_width:\n            current = candidate\n        else:\n            lines.append(current)\n            current = w\n    lines.append(current)\n    return lines\n\n\ndef render_page(draw, font, page_lines, y_start):\n    \"\"\"Render a list of lines onto the draw surface, returning the final y.\"\"\"\n    y = y_start\n    for line in page_lines:\n        if line:\n            draw.text((MARGIN, y), line, fill=\"black\", font=font)\n        y += LINE_HEIGHT\n    return y\n\n\ndef make_pdf_and_ground_truth(all_lines, page_count, output_stem, font):\n    \"\"\"Render the first `page_count` pages and produce an image PDF + JSON.\"\"\"\n    pages_img = []\n    ground_truth = []\n\n    draw = None\n    current_page_words = []\n    lines_this_page = 0\n    max_lines = (H - MARGIN) // LINE_HEIGHT  # ~33 lines per page\n\n    for line in all_lines:\n        if lines_this_page == 0:\n            img = Image.new(\"RGB\", (W, H), \"white\")\n            draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img)\n            current_page_words = []\n\n        if line:\n            render_page(draw, font, [line], MARGIN + lines_this_page * LINE_HEIGHT)\n            current_page_words.extend(line.split())\n\n        lines_this_page += 1\n\n        if lines_this_page >= max_lines:\n            pages_img.append(img)\n            ground_truth.append({\"page\": len(ground_truth) + 1, \"words\": current_page_words})\n            lines_this_page = 0\n\n            if len(pages_img) >= page_count:\n                break\n\n    # Flush partial page if not full\n    if lines_this_page > 0 and len(pages_img) < page_count and draw is not None:\n        pages_img.append(img)\n        ground_truth.append({\"page\": len(ground_truth) + 1, \"words\": current_page_words})\n\n    if not pages_img:\n        print(f\"    Warning: no pages generated for {output_stem}\")\n        return\n\n    # Truncate to requested page count\n    pages_img = pages_img[:page_count]\n    ground_truth = ground_truth[:page_count]\n\n    # Save PDF\n    pdf_path = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, output_stem + \".pdf\")\n    doc = fitz.open()\n    for img in pages_img:\n        buf = io.BytesIO()\n        img.save(buf, format=\"JPEG\", quality=95)\n        buf.seek(0)\n        pix = fitz.Pixmap(buf.read())\n        page = doc.new_page(width=W, height=H)\n        page.insert_image(fitz.Rect(0, 0, W, H), pixmap=pix)\n    doc.save(pdf_path)\n    doc.close()\n    print(f\"    {pdf_path} ({len(pages_img)} pages, {sum(len(p['words']) for p in ground_truth)} words)\")\n\n    # Save ground truth JSON\n    json_path = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, output_stem + \".json\")\n    with open(json_path, \"w\", encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\n        json.dump({\n            \"source\": GUTENBERG_URL,\n            \"title\": \"Moby Dick\",\n            \"pages\": ground_truth,\n        }, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)\n    print(f\"    {json_path}\")\n\n\ndef generate(force=False):\n    for pc in PAGE_COUNTS:\n        pdf_path = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, f\"moby-dick-{pc:03d}p.pdf\")\n        if os.path.exists(pdf_path) and not force:\n            print(f\"  {pdf_path} exists, skipping (use --force to regenerate)\")\n\n    text = download_text(GUTENBERG_URL)\n    text = strip_gutenberg_boilerplate(text)\n    print(f\"  Corpus length: {len(text)} chars\")\n\n    lines = [clean_line(l) for l in text.split(\"\\n\")]\n    lines = [l for l in lines if l is not None]\n\n    # Build a flat list of printable lines (non-empty)\n    printable_lines = [l for l in lines if l]\n\n    empty_img = Image.new(\"RGB\", (W, H), \"white\")\n    empty_draw = ImageDraw.Draw(empty_img)\n    font = get_font()\n\n    total_available = len(printable_lines)\n    max_lines_per_page = (H - MARGIN) // LINE_HEIGHT\n    total_pages_available = total_available // max_lines_per_page + 1\n    print(f\"  Text yields ~{total_pages_available} pages at {max_lines_per_page} lines/page\")\n\n    for pc in PAGE_COUNTS:\n        output_stem = f\"moby-dick-{pc:03d}p\"\n        pdf_path = os.path.join(OUT_DIR, output_stem + \".pdf\")\n        if os.path.exists(pdf_path) and not force:\n            print(f\"  {output_stem} exists, skipping\")\n            continue\n        if pc > total_pages_available:\n            print(f\"  Warning: requested {pc} pages but corpus has only ~{total_pages_available}; truncating\")\n        make_pdf_and_ground_truth(printable_lines, min(pc, total_pages_available), output_stem, font)\n\n    print(\"Done.\")\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=\"Generate OCR evaluation corpus\")\n    parser.add_argument(\"--force\", action=\"store_true\", help=\"Regenerate existing files\")\n    args = parser.parse_args()\n    generate(force=args.force)\n"
}
```

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The corpus is good. Now let me create the Java files.

1. Levenshtein.java - a simple utility with word-level and character-level Levenshtein distance, plus WER/CER computation
2. OcrAccuracyTest.java - the actual test

Let me think about the structure of OcrAccuracyTest:

1. Load ground truth JSON
2. For each eval PDF:
   a. Extract pages using PageExtractor
   b. For each page, run OCREngine.ocr()
   c. Compare OCR words to ground truth words
   d. Compute WER, CER, recall, precision, mean confidence
   e. Report per-page and aggregate

For WER/CER computation:
- WER = Levenshtein distance between OCR word sequence and ground truth word sequence / total ground truth words
- CER = Levenshtein distance between OCR char sequence and ground truth char sequence / total ground truth chars
- Recall = ground truth words found in OCR output / total ground truth words
- Precision = OCR words found in ground truth / total OCR words

Wait, for recall and precision at the word level, I need to think about this more carefully. "Ground truth words found in OCR output" - this is set-based, not sequence-based. Let me use a simple approach: a word from ground truth is "found" if it appears anywhere in the OCR output (case-insensitive). Similarly for precision.

Actually, that's too loose. Let me use a more rigorous approach: I'll compute WER (which is edit-distance-based and accounts for substitutions, insertions, deletions) as the primary metric, and also report the raw matched-word count as a secondary signal.

For the per-page outlier detection, I'll flag pages where WER > 5%.
