<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Excellent! The eval test now PASSES and produces the full summary! 

Results for the 10-page Moby Dick corpus:
- Pages 1-4: High WER (68%, 61%, 72%, 18%) - Title page, ETYMOLOGY, EXTRACTS sections with unusual formatting
- Pages 5-10: Low WER (0.9% - 3.2%) - Regular book text
- Aggregate WER: 15.1% (dragged up by the first 4 pages)
- Aggregate CER: 11.1%
- Word recall: 98.7% (excellent - almost all words found)
- Word precision: 98.7% (excellent - almost no false positives)
- Mean confidence: 92.8
- Average time per page: 84.79 seconds (quite slow due to 300 DPI rendering)

The high WER on pages 1-4 is expected since they contain non-standard text structures (title page, table of contents, etymology tables in multiple languages, etc.)

Now let me update the todo list and present the results.

Let me also re-run all unit tests one more time to confirm everything is clean.

All 20 unit tests pass and the 10-page eval test produces correct results. Here's the evaluation summary for the 10-page Moby Dick corpus:

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Aggregate WER | 15.1% (4 pages of 10 flagged >5% — title/ToC/extracts) |
| Pages with WER < 5% (regular text) | 0.9%–3.2% |
| Aggregate CER | 11.1% |
| Word recall | 98.7% |
| Word precision | 98.7% |
| Mean confidence | 92.8 |
| Time per page | ~85s at 300 DPI |

The 4 flagged pages are the title page, etymology table (multiple languages), and extracts section — all non-standard text layouts. Regular prose pages consistently score <3.2% WER, well within the <5% target.

To run all four corpus sizes (10/50/200/500 pages):
```
./gradlew testEval
```
Expect several hours for 500 pages at current Tesseract speed (~85s/page). Evaluations for 50/200/500 pages are separate `@Test` methods so each runs independently and partial results are visible.

1: # TrulyFreeOCR — Evaluation Plan & Results
2: 
3: ## 1. Ground Truth Corpus
4: 
5: Generated by `tests/generate_eval_corpus.py` from public-domain text (Project Gutenberg — Moby Dick).
6: 
7: | Corpus PDF | Pages | GT Words | Notes |
8: |---|---|---|---|
9: | `moby-dick-010p.pdf` | 10 | 2,422 | Quick smoke-test |
10: | `moby-dick-050p.pdf` | 50 | 17,520 | Short document |
11: | `moby-dick-200p.pdf` | 200 | 73,737 | Typical book chapter |
12: | `moby-dick-500p.pdf` | 500 | 185,776 | Full-length novel |
13: 
14: The generator renders clean image-only PDFs (no selectable text) with a serif font
15: at 300 DPI. Per-page word lists are saved alongside each PDF as JSON ground truth.
16: 
17: To regenerate:
18: ```bash
19: python3 tests/generate_eval_corpus.py          # create if missing
20: python3 tests/generate_eval_corpus.py --force   # re-download + overwrite
21: ```
22: 
23: ## 2. OCR Accuracy Metrics
24: 
25: | Metric | Definition | Target (clean) |
26: |---|---|---|
27: | **Word Error Rate (WER)** | Levenshtein distance at word level / total words | < 5% |
28: | **Character Error Rate (CER)** | Levenshtein distance at character level / total chars | < 2% |
29: | **Word Recall** | Ground-truth words appearing in OCR output | > 95% |
30: | **Word Precision** | OCR output words appearing in ground truth | > 90% |
31: | **Mean Confidence** | Average Tesseract per-word confidence | > 80 |
32: 
33: Implemented in `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/Levenshtein.java` (static helpers)
34: and `OcrAccuracyTest.java` (per-page + aggregate report).
35: 
36: ## 3. Segmentation Quality
37: 
38: | Metric | How |
39: |---|---|
40: | **Blank page false positives** | Foreground pixel % on `blank.pdf` (already tested: < 5%) |
41: | **Text page recall** | Are all rendered text pixels captured in the mask? |
42: | **Inpaint quality** | % of foreground pixels that differ between original and cleaned background (already tested: > 50%) |
43: 
44: ## 4. End-to-End Pipeline
45: 
46: | Test | Verification |
47: |---|---|
48: | **Searchability** | Extract text from output PDF, compare to ground truth per page |
49: | **Compression ratio** | Output / input file size, with and without jbig2enc |
50: | **Metadata round-trip** | Bookmark count, annotation count, document info fields |
51: | **CCITT G4 fallback** | Output size and quality when jbig2enc is unavailable |
52: 
53: ## 5. Parameter Sensitivity (Future Work)
54: 
55: Run pipeline on `moby-dick-010p.pdf` varying one parameter at a time.
56: 
57: | Parameter | Values to Test | Current Default |
58: |---|---|---|
59: | `rendering.dpi` | 150, 200, **300**, 400, 600 | 300 |
60: | `segmenter.tileSize` | 16, 32, **64**, 128, 256 | 64 |
61: | `segmenter.percentile` | 0.85, 0.90, **0.95**, 0.98 | 0.95 |
62: | `segmenter.inpaintRadius` | 1, **3**, 5, 7 | 3 |
63: 
64: ## 6. Performance Baseline (Future Work)
65: 
66: | Stage | What to Time |
67: |---|---|
68: | PageExtractor | Render all pages at 300 DPI |
69: | ImageSegmenter | Grayscale → normalize → Otsu → inpaint |
70: | OCREngine | Tesseract subprocess (TSV output) |
71: | PDFAssembler | Background + mask + text layers |
72: | **Total** | Full pipeline wall-clock |
73: 
74: ## 7. Deliverables
75: 
76: ### Done
77: - `tests/generate_eval_corpus.py` — downloads Moby Dick, renders PDFs, writes JSON ground truth
78: - `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/Levenshtein.java` — WER/CER/recall/precision helpers
79: - `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/GroundTruth.java` — lightweight JSON parser for ground truth format
80: - `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/OcrAccuracyTest.java` — end-to-end eval on all 4 corpus sizes
81: - This file — updated methodology and results summary
82: 
83: ### Future

85: - `ParameterSweepTest.java` — DPI / tile / percentile / radius sweep with WER results
86: - `PerformanceBenchmark.java` — per-stage timing

(End of file - total 86 lines)
</content>

Per-page word lists are saved alongside each PDF as JSON ground truth.\n\nTo regenerate:\n```bash\npython3 tests/generate_eval_corpus.py          # create if missing\npython3 tests/generate_eval_corpus.py --force   # re-download + overwrite\n```\n\n## 2. OCR Accuracy Metrics\n\n| Metric | Definition | Target (clean) |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Word Error Rate (WER)** | Levenshtein distance at word level / total words | < 5% |\n| **Character Error Rate (CER)** | Levenshtein distance at character level / total chars | < 2% |\n| **Word Recall** | Ground-truth words appearing in OCR output | > 95% |\n| **Word Precision** | OCR output words appearing in ground truth | > 90% |\n| **Mean Confidence** | Average Tesseract per-word confidence | > 80 |\n\nImplemented in `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/Levenshtein.java` (static helpers)\nand `OcrAccuracyTest.java` (per-page + aggregate report).\n\n## 3. Segmentation Quality\n\n| Metric | How |\n|---|---|\n| **Blank page false positives** | Foreground pixel % on `blank.pdf` (already tested: < 5%) |\n| **Text page recall** | Are all rendered text pixels captured in the mask? |\n| **Inpaint quality** | % of foreground pixels that differ between original and cleaned background (already tested: > 50%) |\n\n## 4. End-to-End Pipeline\n\n| Test | Verification |\n|---|---|\n| **Searchability** | Extract text from output PDF, compare to ground truth per page |\n| **Compression ratio** | Output / input file size, with and without jbig2enc |\n| **Metadata round-trip** | Bookmark count, annotation count, document info fields |\n| **CCITT G4 fallback** | Output size and quality when jbig2enc is unavailable |\n\n## 5. Parameter Sensitivity (Future Work)\n\nRun pipeline on `moby-dick-010p.pdf` varying one parameter at a time.\n\n| Parameter | Values to Test | Current Default |\n|---|---|---|\n| `rendering.dpi` | 150, 200, **300**, 400, 600 | 300 |\n| `segmenter.tileSize` | 16, 32, **64**, 128, 256 | 64 |\n| `segmenter.percentile` | 0.85, 0.90, **0.95**, 0.98 | 0.95 |\n| `segmenter.inpaintRadius` | 1, **3**, 5, 7 | 3 |\n\n## 6. Performance Baseline (Future Work)\n\n| Stage | What to Time |\n|---|---|\n| PageExtractor | Render all pages at 300 DPI |\n| ImageSegmenter | Grayscale → normalize → Otsu → inpaint |\n| OCREngine | Tesseract subprocess (TSV output) |\n| PDFAssembler | Background + mask + text layers |\n| **Total** | Full pipeline wall-clock |\n\n## 7. Deliverables\n\n### Done\n- `tests/generate_eval_corpus.py` — downloads Moby Dick, renders PDFs, writes JSON ground truth\n- `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/Levenshtein.java` — WER/CER/recall/precision helpers\n- `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/GroundTruth.java` — lightweight JSON parser for ground truth format\n- `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/OcrAccuracyTest.java` — end-to-end eval on all 4 corpus sizes\n- This file — updated methodology and results summary\n\n### Future\n- `SegmentationEvalTest.java` — foreground pixel coverage, inpaint quality\n- `ParameterSweepTest.java` — DPI / tile / percentile / radius sweep with WER results\n- `PerformanceBenchmark.java` — per-stage timing",
  "newString": "# TrulyFreeOCR — Evaluation Plan & Results\n\n## 1. Ground Truth Corpus\n\nGenerated by `tests/generate_eval_corpus.py` from public-domain text (Project Gutenberg — Moby Dick).\n\n| Corpus PDF | Pages | GT Words | Notes |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| `moby-dick-010p.pdf` | 10 | 2,422 | Quick smoke-test |\n| `moby-dick-050p.pdf` | 50 | 17,520 | Short document |\n| `moby-dick-200p.pdf` | 200 | 73,737 | Typical book chapter |\n| `moby-dick-500p.pdf` | 500 | 185,776 | Full-length novel |\n\nThe generator renders clean image-only PDFs (no selectable text) with a serif font\nat 300 DPI. Per-page word lists are saved alongside each PDF as JSON ground truth.\n\nTo regenerate:\n```bash\npython3 tests/generate_eval_corpus.py          # create if missing\npython3 tests/generate_eval_corpus.py --force   # re-download + overwrite\n```\n\n## 2. OCR Accuracy Metrics\n\n| Metric | Definition | Target (clean) |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Word Error Rate (WER)** | Levenshtein distance at word level / total words | < 5% |\n| **Character Error Rate (CER)** | Levenshtein distance at character level / total chars | < 2% |\n| **Word Recall** | Ground-truth words appearing in OCR output | > 95% |\n| **Word Precision** | OCR output words appearing in ground truth | > 90% |\n| **Mean Confidence** | Average Tesseract per-word confidence | > 80 |\n\nImplemented in `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/Levenshtein.java` (static helpers)\nand `OcrAccuracyTest.java` (per-page + aggregate report).\n\n## 3. Current Results\n\n### 10-Page Corpus (`moby-dick-010p.pdf`, 10 pages, 2,412 normalized GT words)\n\n| Page | Content | WER | CER | Recall | Precision | Confidence | Time |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 1 | Title / Contents | 67.8% | 49.4% | 98.3% | 99.2% | 93.2 | 44s |\n| 2 | Etymology (multi-lang) | 61.4% | 49.1% | 97.5% | 97.5% | 93.8 | 86s |\n| 3 | Extracts (mixed quotes) | 71.7% | 54.5% | 96.4% | 96.4% | 93.1 | 75s |\n| 4 | Extracts (mixed quotes) | 17.5% | 12.5% | 99.4% | 99.4% | 94.7 | 74s |\n| 5 | Chapter 1 prose | 1.2% | 0.2% | 99.2% | 99.6% | 93.9 | 111s |\n| 6 | Chapter 1 prose | 3.2% | 1.2% | 98.2% | 97.7% | 89.5 | 75s |\n| 7 | Chapter 1 prose | 0.9% | 0.2% | 99.1% | 99.1% | 93.5 | 81s |\n| 8 | Chapter 1 prose | 1.3% | 0.2% | 99.4% | 99.1% | 92.3 | 75s |\n| 9 | Chapter 1 prose | 3.0% | 0.4% | 97.9% | 98.2% | 92.6 | 136s |\n| 10 | Chapter 1 prose | 3.2% | 0.8% | 97.4% | 99.1% | 92.8 | 91s |\n\n**Aggregate (10 pages):**\n- Aggregate WER: 15.1%\n- Aggregate CER: 11.1%\n- Word recall: 98.7%\n- Word precision: 98.7%\n- Mean confidence: 92.8\n- Total time: ~848s (~85s/page)\n- Pages with WER > 5%: 4 / 10 (title, etymology, extracts — non-standard layouts)\n\n### Analysis\n\n- **Regular prose pages** (pages 5–10) achieve **WER 0.9%–3.2%**, well within the <5% target. CER is <1.2%.\n- **Title, etymology, and extracts pages** (pages 1–4, ~40% of this corpus) inflate the aggregate due to multi-column layouts, mixed languages, punctuation-heavy extracts, and unusual typography. These pages are not representative of typical document content.\n- **Recall and precision** are excellent across all pages (≥96%), indicating Tesseract correctly identifies almost all words and introduces very few false positives.\n- **Mean confidence** is high and stable (89.5–94.7).\n- **Per-page time** varies 44–136s at 300 DPI, averaging ~85s. Expected to scale linearly with page count.\n\n### Larger Corpora (Not Yet Evaluated)\n\n| Corpus | Pages | GT Words | Est. Time (85s/page) | Status |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `moby-dick-050p.pdf` | 50 | 17,520 | ~71 min | Pending |\n| `moby-dick-200p.pdf` | 200 | 73,737 | ~4.7 hr | Pending |\n| `moby-dick-500p.pdf` | 500 | 185,776 | ~11.8 hr | Pending |\n\nRun with: `./gradlew testEval`\n\n## 4. Segmentation Quality\n\n| Metric | How |\n|---|---|\n| **Blank page false positives** | Foreground pixel % on `blank.pdf` (already tested: < 5%) |\n| **Text page recall** | Are all rendered text pixels captured in the mask? |\n| **Inpaint quality** | % of foreground pixels that differ between original and cleaned background (already tested: > 50%) |\n\n## 5. End-to-End Pipeline\n\n| Test | Verification |\n|---|---|\n| **Searchability** | Extract text from output PDF, compare to ground truth per page |\n| **Compression ratio** | Output / input file size, with and without jbig2enc |\n| **Metadata round-trip** | Bookmark count, annotation count, document info fields |\n| **CCITT G4 fallback** | Output size and quality when jbig2enc is unavailable |\n\n## 6. Parameter Sensitivity (Future Work)\n\nRun pipeline on `moby-dick-010p.pdf` varying one parameter at a time.\n\n| Parameter | Values to Test | Current Default |\n|---|---|---|\n| `rendering.dpi` | 150, 200, **300**, 400, 600 | 300 |\n| `segmenter.tileSize` | 16, 32, **64**, 128, 256 | 64 |\n| `segmenter.percentile` | 0.85, 0.90, **0.95**, 0.98 | 0.95 |\n| `segmenter.inpaintRadius` | 1, **3**, 5, 7 | 3 |\n\n## 7. Performance Baseline (Future Work)\n\n| Stage | What to Time |\n|---|---|\n| PageExtractor | Render all pages at 300 DPI |\n| ImageSegmenter | Grayscale → normalize → Otsu → inpaint |\n| OCREngine | Tesseract subprocess (TSV output) |\n| PDFAssembler | Background + mask + text layers |\n| **Total** | Full pipeline wall-clock |\n\n## 8. Deliverables\n\n### Done\n- `tests/generate_eval_corpus.py` — downloads Moby Dick, renders PDFs, writes JSON ground truth\n- `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/Levenshtein.java` — WER/CER/recall/precision helpers\n- `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/GroundTruth.java` — lightweight JSON parser for ground truth format\n- `src/test/java/com/trulyfreeocr/eval/OcrAccuracyTest.java` — end-to-end eval on all 4 corpus sizes\n- This file — methodology, results, and analysis\n\n### Future\n- `SegmentationEvalTest.java` — foreground pixel coverage, inpaint quality\n- `ParameterSweepTest.java` — DPI / tile / percentile / radius sweep with WER results\n- `PerformanceBenchmark.java` — per-stage timing\n- Run larger corpora (50/200/500 pages) and populate results table"

1: package com.trulyfreeocr.pipeline;
2: 
3: import java.awt.image.BufferedImage;
4: import java.io.File;
5: import java.io.IOException;
6: import java.util.ArrayList;
7: import java.util.List;

9: import org.apache.pdfbox.Loader;
10: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.PDDocument;
11: import org.apache.pdfbox.rendering.PDFRenderer;
12: 
13: /**
14:  * Renders each page of a PDF to a BufferedImage at 300 DPI
15:  * using PDFBox's PDFRenderer.
16:  *
17:  * 300 DPI is the standard scanning resolution for OCR:
18:  *   - US Letter (8.5"x11")  → 2550 × 3300 px
19:  *   - A4 (210×297 mm)        → 2480 × 3508 px
20:  *
21:  * The renderer uses PDFBox's built-in SFF (subpixel font finalizer)
22:  * for high-quality text rendering.
23:  *
24:  * Implements AutoCloseable so the underlying PDDocument is released.
25:  * Callers should use try-with-resources.
26:  */
27: public class PageExtractor implements AutoCloseable {
28: 
29:     private PDDocument document;
30:     private final float dpi;
31: 
32:     public PageExtractor() {
33:         this(300f);
34:     }
35: 
36:     public PageExtractor(float dpi) {
37:         this.dpi = dpi;
38:     }
39: 
40:     /**
41:      * Loads a PDF and renders every page.
42:      *
43:      * @param pdf  Input PDF file.
44:      * @return List of BufferedImages, one per page, at the configured DPI.
45:      */
46:     public List<BufferedImage> extractPages(File pdf) throws IOException {
47:         document = Loader.loadPDF(pdf);
48:         PDFRenderer renderer = new PDFRenderer(document);
49:         int pageCount = document.getNumberOfPages();
50:         List<BufferedImage> pages = new ArrayList<>(pageCount);
51:         for (int i = 0; i < pageCount; i++) {
52:             BufferedImage img = renderer.renderImageWithDPI(i, dpi);
53:             pages.add(img);
54:         }
55:         return pages;
56:     }
57: 
58:     @Override
59:     public void close() throws IOException {
60:         if (document != null) {
61:             document.close();
62:         }
63:     }
64: }

(End of file - total 64 lines)

<type>file</type>
<content>
1: package com.trulyfreeocr.pipeline;
2: 
3: import java.awt.Rectangle;
4: import java.awt.image.BufferedImage;
5: import java.io.BufferedReader;
6: import java.io.File;
7: import java.io.IOException;
8: import java.io.InputStreamReader;
9: import java.util.ArrayList;
10: import java.util.List;
11: 
12: import javax.imageio.ImageIO;
13: 
14: import com.trulyfreeocr.model.PageResult;
15: import com.trulyfreeocr.model.TextBlock;
16: 
17: /**
18:  * OCR engine that delegates to the Tesseract CLI via subprocess.
19:  *
20:  * Tess4J's JNA binding was originally planned, but lept4j's
21:  * Leptonica version requires symbols not present in the system's
22:  * liblept.so.5.  Running tesseract as a subprocess (with TSV output)
23:  * avoids this native-library version mismatch entirely and is more
24:  * portable across Linux distributions.
25:  *
26:  * Tesseract output format: TSV (tab-separated values) with columns:
27:  *   level, page_num, block_num, par_num, line_num, word_num,
28:  *   left, top, width, height, conf, text
29:  *
30:  * CLI flags used:
31:  *   -l eng    — English language model
32:  *   --psm 1   — Automatic page segmentation with OSD
33:  *   tsv       — Output format: TSV (word-level bounding boxes)
34:  *
35:  * The output PageResult always reports pageNumber=1 because each
36:  * call processes exactly one BufferedImage.
37:  */
38: public class OCREngine {
39: 
40:     private final File tessdataDir;
41:     private final String tesseractPath;
42:     private final String language;
43:     private final String psm;
44: 
45:     public OCREngine() {
46:         this("tessdata", "native/linux/tesseract", "eng", "1");
47:     }
48: 
49:     public OCREngine(String tessdataDir, String tesseractPath, String language, String psm) {
50:         this.tesseractPath = tesseractPath;
51:         this.language = language;
52:         this.psm = psm;
53: 
54:         if (tessdataDir != null && !tessdataDir.isEmpty()) {
55:             this.tessdataDir = new File(tessdataDir);
56:         } else {
57:             String env = System.getenv("TESSDATA_PREFIX");
58:             if (env != null && !env.isEmpty()) {
59:                 this.tessdataDir = new File(env);
60:             } else {
61:                 this.tessdataDir = new File("/usr/share/tesseract-ocr/5/tessdata");
62:             }
63:         }
64:     }
65: 
66:     public PageResult ocr(BufferedImage image) throws IOException {
67:         int width = image.getWidth();
68:         int height = image.getHeight();
69: 
70:         // Write image to temp file
71:         File tempPng = File.createTempFile("tfocr-", ".png");
72:         tempPng.deleteOnExit();
73:         ImageIO.write(image, "png", tempPng);
74: 
75:         try {
76:             // Run tesseract with TSV output for word-level bounding boxes
77:             String prefix = tempPng.getAbsolutePath().replaceAll("\\.png$", "");
78:             ProcessBuilder pb = new ProcessBuilder(
79:                     tesseractPath,
80:                     tempPng.getAbsolutePath(),
81:                     prefix,
82:                     "-l", language,
83:                     "--psm", psm,
84:                     "tsv"
85:             );
86:             pb.environment().put("TESSDATA_PREFIX", tessdataDir.getAbsolutePath());
87:             pb.redirectErrorStream(true);
88: 
89:             Process process = pb.start();
90:             try (BufferedReader reader = new BufferedReader(
91:                     new InputStreamReader(process.getInputStream()))) {
92:                 // Read and discard stdout (status messages go here)
93:                 while (reader.readLine() != null) { }
94:             }
95:             boolean exited = process.waitFor(30, java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit.SECONDS);
96:             if (!exited) {
97:                 process.destroyForcibly();
98:                 throw new IOException("Tesseract timed out after 30 seconds");
99:             }
100:             int exitCode = process.exitValue();
101:             if (exitCode != 0) {
102:                 throw new IOException("Tesseract exited with code " + exitCode);
103:             }
104: 
105:             // Parse TSV output
106:             File tsvFile = new File(prefix + ".tsv");
107:             if (!tsvFile.exists()) {
108:                 return new PageResult(1, width, height, List.of());
109:             }
110: 
111:             List<TextBlock> blocks = parseTsv(tsvFile);
112:             return new PageResult(1, width, height, blocks);
113: 
114:         } catch (InterruptedException e) {
115:             Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
116:             throw new IOException("Tesseract was interrupted", e);
117:         } finally {
118:             // Cleanup temp files
119:             String prefix = tempPng.getAbsolutePath().replaceAll("\\.png$", "");
120:             for (String ext : new String[]{".png", ".tsv"}) {
121:                 File f = new File(prefix + ext);
122:                 if (f.exists()) f.delete();
123:             }
124:         }
125:     }
126: 
127:     private List<TextBlock> parseTsv(File tsv) throws IOException {
128:         List<TextBlock> blocks = new ArrayList<>();
129:         try (BufferedReader reader = new BufferedReader(new java.io.FileReader(tsv))) {
130:             String header = reader.readLine(); // skip header
131:             if (header == null) return blocks;
132: 
133:             String line;
134:             while ((line = reader.readLine()) != null) {
135:                 if (line.trim().isEmpty()) continue;
136:                 String[] parts = line.split("\t");
137:                 // TSV columns: level, page_num, block_num, par_num, line_num, word_num,
138:                 //              left, top, width, height, conf, text
139:                 if (parts.length < 12) continue;
140: 
141:                 String level = parts[0];
142:                 String text = parts[11].trim();
143:                 if (!"5".equals(level) || text.isEmpty()) continue; // word level only
144: 
145:                 try {
146:                     int left = Integer.parseInt(parts[6]);
147:                     int top = Integer.parseInt(parts[7]);
148:                     int w = Integer.parseInt(parts[8]);
149:                     int h = Integer.parseInt(parts[9]);
150:                     double conf = Double.parseDouble(parts[10]);
151: 
152:                     blocks.add(new TextBlock(text, new Rectangle(left, top, w, h), conf));
153:                 } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
154:                     // skip malformed lines
155:                 }
156:             }
157:         }
158:         return blocks;
159:     }
160: }

(End of file - total 160 lines)
</content>
```

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now let me analyze the bottleneck. The test runs:
1. `PageExtractor.extractPages()` — renders PDF to BufferedImages at 300 DPI via PDFBox
2. `OCREngine.ocr()` for each page — writes temp PNG, runs Tesseract subprocess, parses TSV

Let me check whether the extractor is being called per-page or once. Looking at the test code, the extractor is called once to get all pages, then OCR is called per page. So the extraction time is amortized.

Let me benchmark each step quickly by using `time` to see how long Tesseract takes vs rendering.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The key observations:

1. **Tesseract at 300 DPI**: Each page is ~2550×3300 px. Tesseract has to process this full-resolution image, which involves:
   - Loading the PNG (memory + decode time)
   - Page layout analysis (PSM = 1 includes orientation/script detection)
   - Character recognition
   - Output generation (TSV)

2. **PSM 1 with OSD**: Using `--psm 1` enables automatic page segmentation with orientation and script detection. This adds significant overhead because Tesseract first analyzes the page layout to detect text orientation, columns, etc. For single-column text like Moby Dick, PSM 3 (fully automatic, no OSD) or PSM 6 (single uniform block) would be much faster.

3. **Subprocess overhead**: Each page spawns a new Tesseract process - process creation overhead (~10-20ms), loading the traineddata files, loading OSD data.

4. **Temp file I/O**: Writing a PNG to disk and then having Tesseract read it back adds I/O overhead.

Let me actually run a quick benchmark to confirm.

</details>

The bottleneck is likely `--psm 1` (OSD). Let me check:

**Input:**

```json

  "command": "pdftoppm -png -r 300 -l 1 tests/eval-corpus/moby-dick-010p.pdf /tmp/tfocr-bench 2>&1 && echo \"--- PSM 1 (current, with OSD) ---\" && time (native/linux/tesseract /tmp/tfocr-bench-1.png /tmp/tfocr-out1 -l eng --psm 1 tsv 2>/dev/null) && echo \"--- PSM 3 (no OSD) ---\" && time (native/linux/tesseract /tmp/tfocr-bench-1.png /tmp/tfocr-out3 -l eng --psm 3 tsv 2>/dev/null) && echo \"--- PSM 6 (single block) ---\" && time (native/linux/tesseract /tmp/tfocr-bench-1.png /tmp/tfocr-out6 -l eng --psm 6 tsv 2>/dev/null) && echo \"--- PNG write time ---\" && time (pdftoppm -png -r 300 -l 1 tests/eval-corpus/moby-dick-010p.pdf /tmp/tfocr-bench2 2>/dev/null) && rm -f /tmp/tfocr-bench-1.png /tmp/tfocr-bench2-1.png /tmp/tfocr-out*",
  "description": "Benchmark PSM modes vs Tesseract speed",
  "timeout": 300000,
  "workdir": "/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR"
}

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Interesting - PSM 1 only takes 0.95s for a single page when run directly from the command line. But the test shows 44-136s per page. Something else is going on. The overhead must be elsewhere - maybe the PNG generation via PDFBox is slow (it does Java rendering, not pdftoppm), or there's overhead in the Java subprocess startup.

Let me check what the image dimensions are and if there are multiple copies of the image being made.

Wait - looking at the OCREngine code, it writes the BufferedImage to a temp PNG file, then runs Tesseract on it. The `ImageIO.write()` call could be slow for a large 300 DPI image, but that shouldn't take minutes.

Actually, the issue might be that in the test, we're not just running OCR - we also have to render the PDF pages via PDFBox's `renderImageWithDPI()`. PDFBox rendering of a full page at 300 DPI can be quite slow in Java, especially for the first page when fonts need to be loaded.

Let me benchmark the actual steps used in the test: PDFBox rendering.

</details>

### Tool: bash

**Input:**

```json
{
  "command": "cat > /tmp/BenchEval.java << 'JAVA'\nimport java.io.*;\nimport java.awt.image.BufferedImage;\nimport javax.imageio.ImageIO;\nimport org.apache.pdfbox.Loader;\nimport org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.PDDocument;\nimport org.apache.pdfbox.rendering.PDFRenderer;\n\npublic class BenchEval {\n    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {\n        File pdf = new File(\"tests/eval-corpus/moby-dick-010p.pdf\");\n\n        long t0 = System.currentTimeMillis();\n        try (PDDocument doc = Loader.loadPDF(pdf)) {\n            PDFRenderer renderer = new PDFRenderer(doc);\n            int n = doc.getNumberOfPages();\n            System.out.println(\"Pages: \" + n);\n            long renderTotal = 0, pngTotal = 0, tessTotal = 0;\n            for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {\n                long r0 = System.currentTimeMillis();\n                BufferedImage img = renderer.renderImageWithDPI(i, 300);\n                long r1 = System.currentTimeMillis();\n                File tmp = File.createTempFile(\"tfocr-\", \".png\");\n                ImageIO.write(img, \"png\", tmp);\n                long r2 = System.currentTimeMillis();\n\n                ProcessBuilder pb = new ProcessBuilder(\n                    \"native/linux/tesseract\", tmp.getAbsolutePath(),\n                    tmp.getAbsolutePath().replaceAll(\"\\\\.png$\", \"\"),\n                    \"-l\", \"eng\", \"--psm\", \"1\", \"tsv\"\n                );\n                pb.environment().put(\"TESSDATA_PREFIX\", new File(\"tessdata\").getAbsolutePath());\n                pb.redirectErrorStream(true);\n                Process proc = pb.start();\n                try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(proc.getInputStream()))) {\n                    while (br.readLine() != null) {}\n                }\n                proc.waitFor();\n                long r3 = System.currentTimeMillis();\n\n                renderTotal += r1 - r0;\n                pngTotal += r2 - r1;\n                tessTotal += r3 - r2;\n                tmp.delete();\n                new File(tmp.getAbsolutePath().replaceAll(\"\\\\.png$\", \".tsv\")).delete();\n\n                System.out.printf(\"Page %2d: render=%4dms  png=%4dms  tesseract=%4dms%n\",\n                    i+1, r1-r0, r2-r1, r3-r2);\n            }\n            long t1 = System.currentTimeMillis();\n            System.out.printf(\"%nTotal: %dms  Render: %dms  PNG: %dms  Tesseract: %dms%n\",\n                t1-t0, renderTotal, pngTotal, tessTotal);\n        }\n    }\n}\nJAVA\n# Compile and run using the project's classpath\nCLASSPATH=$(find build/libs -name \"*.jar\" 2>/dev/null | tr '\\n' ':')$(find /home/bgu/.gradle/caches -name \"pdfbox-3.0.6.jar\" 2>/dev/null | head -1)$(find /home/bgu/.gradle/caches -name \"fontbox-3.0.6.jar\" 2>/dev/null | head -1)\necho \"Classpath: $CLASSPATH\"\njavac -cp \"build/classes/java/main:$CLASSPATH\" /tmp/BenchEval.java -d /tmp/bench 2>&1 && java -cp \"/tmp/bench:build/classes/java/main:$CLASSPATH\" BenchEval 2>&1",
  "description": "Benchmark PDFBox rendering + PNG + Tesseract",
  "timeout": 300000,
  "workdir": "/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR"
}
```

{
  "command": "cat > /tmp/PerfTest.java << 'JAVA'\npackage com.trulyfreeocr.eval;\n\nimport java.io.*;\nimport java.awt.image.BufferedImage;\nimport javax.imageio.ImageIO;\nimport com.trulyfreeocr.pipeline.PageExtractor;\n\nclass PerfTest {\n    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {\n        File pdf = new File(\"tests/eval-corpus/moby-dick-010p.pdf\");\n\n        long t0 = System.nanoTime();\n        PageExtractor pe = new PageExtractor(300);\n        var pages = pe.extractPages(pdf);\n        pe.close();\n        long t1 = System.nanoTime();\n        System.out.printf(\"PageExtractor (%d pages): %.3f s%n\", pages.size(), (t1-t0)/1e9);\n\n        int idx = 0; // first page\n        BufferedImage img = pages.get(idx);\n\n        long t2 = System.nanoTime();\n        File tmp = File.createTempFile(\"tfocr-\", \".png\");\n        ImageIO.write(img, \"png\", tmp);\n        long t3 = System.nanoTime();\n        System.out.printf(\"PNG write: %.3f s (file: %s)%n\", (t3-t2)/1e9, tmp.getAbsolutePath());\n        System.out.printf(\"Image size: %dx%d px, PNG file: %d bytes%n\",\n            img.getWidth(), img.getHeight(), tmp.length());\n\n        long t4 = System.nanoTime();\n        ProcessBuilder pb = new ProcessBuilder(\n            \"native/linux/tesseract\", tmp.getAbsolutePath(),\n            tmp.getAbsolutePath().replaceAll(\"\\\\.png$\", \"\"),\n            \"-l\", \"eng\", \"--psm\", \"1\", \"tsv\"\n        );\n        pb.environment().put(\"TESSDATA_PREFIX\", new java.io.File(\"tessdata\").getAbsolutePath());\n        pb.redirectErrorStream(true);\n        Process proc = pb.start();\n        try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(proc.getInputStream()))) {\n            while (br.readLine() != null) {}\n        }\n        proc.waitFor();\n        long t5 = System.nanoTime();\n        System.out.printf(\"Tesseract (--psm 1): %.3f s%n\", (t5-t4)/1e9);\n\n        // Parse TSV\n        File tsv = new File(tmp.getAbsolutePath().replaceAll(\"\\\\.png$\", \".tsv\"));\n        long count = 0;\n        if (tsv.exists()) {\n            try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new java.io.FileReader(tsv))) {\n                while (br.readLine() != null) count++;\n            }\n        }\n        long t6 = System.nanoTime();\n        System.out.printf(\"TSV parse (%d lines): %.3f s%n\", count, (t6-t5)/1e9);\n\n        tmp.delete();\n        tsv.delete();\n    }\n}\nJAVA\n\n# Compile against the project's classes\njavac -cp \"build/classes/java/main\" /tmp/PerfTest.java -d /tmp/perfbench 2>&1\njava -cp \"/tmp/perfbench:build/classes/java/main\" com.trulyfreeocr.eval.PerfTest 2>&1",
  "description": "Benchmark per-step timing via classpath",
  "timeout": 120000,
  "workdir": "/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR"
}

Actually, `waitFor()` DOES block until the process completes. And we read stdout concurrently. So it should be equivalent to running tesseract from the command line.

So from CLI, tesseract takes 0.655s with the exact same env vars. But from Java, it takes 83.5s. The temp PNG was already deleted by the Java benchmark code.

Let me think about what could be so different. The Java code:
1. Creates a BufferedImage via PDFBox
2. Writes it to a temp PNG file
3. Calls ProcessBuilder.start()

5. Calls waitFor()

Step 4 is interesting - Java reads stdout while the process is running. If Tesseract produces stderr (not stdout when redirectErrorStream is true), reading stdout might block.

Actually, the issue might be the `ProcessBuilder.redirectErrorStream(true)` combined with the stdout reading loop. If Tesseract writes to stderr first (which is redirected to stdout), the stdout reader loop could actually be waiting for more input while Tesseract has already finished its main work and is blocked waiting for the stdout pipe to be drained.

Wait no, `waitFor()` returns when the process exits. The stdout reading loop keeps the pipe drained so the process won't block. This should work correctly.

Unless... the process is not actually exiting quickly. Let me check if maybe there's an issue with the wrapper script `native/linux/tesseract` that causes it to behave differently when called from Java vs CLI.

Interesting! The PDFBox-generated PNG takes **55.5s** with tesseract, but the pdftoppm-generated one takes **0.52s**. Same page, same DPI, different rendering engines!

Wait, actually `pdftoppm -png -r 300 -l 1 tests/eval-corpus/moby-dick-010p.pdf /tmp/tfocr-pp` should produce `/tmp/tfocr-pp-1.png`. But the `ls: cannot access` error means it wasn't found. Maybe pdftoppm named it differently.

But the key finding: PDFBox generates PNGs that Tesseract is 100x slower to process! Why?

Possible reasons:
1. **Anti-aliased text vs bilevel**: PDFBox renders anti-aliased text with subpixel rendering (colored fringes), while pdftoppm produces bilevel (black-and-white) text. Tesseract might process anti-aliased grayscale images much slower.
2. **Color depth**: PDFBox might output 24-bit RGB, while pdftoppm outputs bilevel/gray
3. **Image compression**: PDFBox might generate larger PNGs

Let me check the image formats:

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now I have the full picture:

**Root cause: PDFBox renders at 300 DPI in full 24-bit RGB color with anti-aliased text, which Tesseract processes ~100x slower than bilevel black-and-white images.**

From the analysis:
- PDFBox image: 2550×3299 px, RGB mode (3 channels), 347KB
- pdftoppm image: 2550×3300 px, bilevel (1-bit), ~124KB

Even though the max RG diff is 0 (gray values are equal across channels), Tesseract still sees a 24-bit RGB image which triggers its full pipeline with color processing (deskew, despeckle, etc.) rather than the optimized bilevel path.

The fix is to convert the BufferedImage to grayscale (TYPE_BYTE_GRAY) or binary (TYPE_BYTE_BINARY) before passing it to Tesseract. This would:
1. Drastically speed up Tesseract processing (from ~80s to <1s per page)
2. Possibly improve accuracy since Tesseract works best on clean binary images

Now let me formulate the plan.

The simplest fix: In the OCREngine.ocr() method, convert the BufferedImage to grayscale before writing the PNG. This can be done with a simple helper:

```java
private static BufferedImage toGrayscale(BufferedImage src) {
    BufferedImage gray = new BufferedImage(src.getWidth(), src.getHeight(), BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_GRAY);
    ColorSpace cs = ColorSpace.getInstance(ColorSpace.CS_GRAY);
    ColorConvertOp op = new ColorConvertOp(cs, null);
    op.filter(src, gray);
    return gray;
}
```

Or simpler:
```java
BufferedImage gray = new BufferedImage(img.getWidth(), img.getHeight(), BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_GRAY);
Graphics2D g = gray.createGraphics();
g.drawImage(img, 0, 0, null);
g.dispose();
```

This conversion takes minimal time (<50ms for a 2550×3299 image) and reduces the Tesseract processing time from ~80s to <1s.

Actually, the even better approach might be to use `BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_BINARY` (bilevel) directly, since that's what Tesseract works on best. But we should check if converting to bilevel loses too much quality for OCR. Actually, one approach: keep it in TYPE_BYTE_GRAY which is still grayscale but not bilevel. Tesseract would still process it faster.

Let me propose a plan.

</details>

Here is the breakdown from the benchmark:

| Step | Time (page 1) |
|---|---|
| PageExtractor (PDFBox 300 DPI, all 10 pages) | 5.6s total, ~0.56s/page |
| PNG write (Java ImageIO) | 1.7s |
| **Tesseract CLI subprocess** | **83.5s** |
| TSV parse | 0.003s |

**The bottleneck is Tesseract at 83s/page.** The root cause: **PDFBox renders 24-bit RGB images**, and Tesseract's color processing path is ~100x slower than its bilevel path:

- PDFBox PNG (RGB, 347KB) → Tesseract takes **55s**
- pdftoppm PNG (bilevel, ~124KB) → Tesseract takes **0.5s**

Same page, same 300 DPI, just different color depth.

**Proposed fix**: In `OCREngine.ocr()`, convert the BufferedImage to `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` or `TYPE_BYTE_BINARY` before writing the PNG. This is a ~50ms conversion that would drop Tesseract time from ~80s to <1s per page.

```java
// Convert to grayscale before writing PNG (Tesseract processes bilevel/gray much faster)
BufferedImage gray = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_GRAY);
Graphics2D g = gray.createGraphics();
g.drawImage(image, 0, 0, null);
g.dispose();
ImageIO.write(gray, "png", tempPng);
```

Want me to implement this?

## User

Why it has to use 24-bit RGB to render color for text dominant page? Should 8-bit RGB enough?

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The user is asking a valid question. They're questioning why the current rendering uses 24-bit RGB when the text pages are mostly black and white. They're suggesting that 8-bit RGB (or even grayscale) should be sufficient.

Let me think about this:

1. PDFBox's `renderImageWithDPI()` defaults to producing 24-bit RGB images with anti-aliasing (subpixel rendering). This is the default behavior for maximum visual quality.

2. For OCR purposes, 8-bit grayscale (or even 1-bit binary) is sufficient. Tesseract internally converts to grayscale anyway.

3. The question is about what the FIX should be:
   a. Convert to grayscale in OCREngine before writing PNG (the fix I proposed)
   b. Change PDFBox rendering to produce grayscale directly (PDFBox supports `RenderQuality` settings but not a direct grayscale output mode)
   c. Change the PNG format to be grayscale in ImageIO.write()

The simplest and most correct fix is option (a): convert the image to grayscale in OCREngine before passing to Tesseract. This is because:
- Tesseract internally converts to grayscale anyway
- The conversion is lossless for text (text is still perfectly readable in grayscale)
- It saves significant I/O and processing time

But the user is also pointing out something more fundamental: does the PageExtractor and the whole pipeline really need to work with 24-bit color images? For the pipeline's use case (scanned document OCR), grayscale is sufficient everywhere except possibly in the final output PDF where the original color page should be preserved.

The plan I should present:

1. In OCREngine.ocr(): Convert to grayscale before PNG write. This is the most impactful and least intrusive change.
2. Optionally: In PageExtractor, consider whether to change the default rendering to grayscale. But this is more complex since the image data flows through ImageSegmenter and PDFAssembler, and downstream stages might need color.

Let me present the plan clearly and ask the user what they prefer.

</details>

You're right — for text-dominant pages, 24-bit RGB is overkill. PDFBox defaults to it because it uses subpixel anti-aliasing for on-screen rendering quality, but Tesseract internally converts to grayscale anyway, so the extra two channels are wasted.

Two options for the fix:

**A. Convert to grayscale in OCREngine** (before writing PNG):
- Minimal change, ~50ms conversion cost
- Drop Tesseract time from ~80s → ~0.5s per page
- No impact on other pipeline stages (PageExtractor, ImageSegmenter, PDFAssembler still get full color)

**B. Render at lower color depth in PageExtractor directly** (e.g., `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY`):
- Saves the conversion step, but...
- Downstream stages like `ImageSegmenter` (which does color-to-gray normalization) and `PDFAssembler` (which embeds the background image) might want the original color data for non-text elements (logos, highlights, stamp colors). The pipeline currently preserves the original rendered color page.

I'd recommend **option A** — it's the most surgical fix, requires no pipeline redesign, and gives the same speedup. The grayscale conversion is:

```java
BufferedImage gray = new BufferedImage(w, h, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_GRAY);
Graphics2D g = gray.createGraphics();
g.drawImage(image, 0, 0, null);
g.dispose();
// then ImageIO.write(gray, "png", tempPng)
```

Shall I go with option A, or would you prefer a broader change?

1: package com.trulyfreeocr.util;
2: 
3: import java.io.IOException;
4: import java.nio.file.Files;
5: import java.nio.file.Path;
6: import java.nio.file.Paths;
7: import java.util.HashMap;
8: import java.util.Map;
9: 
10: public class Settings {
11: 
12:     private static Settings instance;
13: 
14:     private final Map<String, String> values;
15: 
16:     private static final Path DEFAULT_PATH = Paths.get("settings.jsonc");
17: 
18:     // System property key for overriding the settings file location.
19:     private static final String PROP_KEY = "tfocr.settings";
20: 
21:     public Settings() {
22:         this.values = new HashMap<>();
23:     }
24: 
25:     public Settings(Map<String, String> values) {
26:         this.values = new HashMap<>(values);
27:     }
28: 
29:     /**
30:      * Loads settings from the default location (settings.jsonc in CWD),
31:      * or from the path specified by the "tfocr.settings" system property.
32:      * Does nothing if the file does not exist — all getters fall back to
33:      * their supplied defaults.
34:      */
35:     public static Settings load() {
36:         Settings s = new Settings();
37:         Path path = findPath();
38:         if (path != null && Files.exists(path)) {
39:             try {
40:                 String raw = Files.readString(path);
41:                 s.values.putAll(parseJsonc(raw));
42:             } catch (IOException e) {
43:                 // file missing or unreadable — use defaults
44:             }
45:         }
46:         instance = s;
47:         return s;
48:     }
49: 
50:     /**
51:      * Returns the singleton instance.  Must be preceded by a call to load().
52:      */
53:     public static Settings getInstance() {
54:         if (instance == null) {
55:             instance = new Settings();
56:         }
57:         return instance;
58:     }
59: 
60:     // ---- typed accessors with defaults ----
61: 
62:     public String getString(String key, String defaultValue) {
63:         return values.getOrDefault(key, defaultValue);
64:     }
65: 
66:     public int getInt(String key, int defaultValue) {
67:         String v = values.get(key);
68:         if (v == null) return defaultValue;
69:         try { return Integer.parseInt(v); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { return defaultValue; }
70:     }
71: 
72:     public double getDouble(String key, double defaultValue) {
73:         String v = values.get(key);
74:         if (v == null) return defaultValue;
75:         try { return Double.parseDouble(v); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { return defaultValue; }
76:     }
77: 
78:     public boolean getBoolean(String key, boolean defaultValue) {
79:         String v = values.get(key);
80:         if (v == null) return defaultValue;
81:         return "true".equalsIgnoreCase(v);
82:     }
83: 
84:     // ---- path resolution ----
85: 
86:     private static Path findPath() {
87:         String prop = System.getProperty(PROP_KEY);
88:         if (prop != null && !prop.isEmpty()) {
89:             return Paths.get(prop);
90:         }
91:         return DEFAULT_PATH;
92:     }
93: 
94:     // ---- minimal JSONC parser (flat objects only) ----
95: 
96:     static Map<String, String> parseJsonc(String raw) {
97:         String stripped = stripComments(raw);
98:         return parseJson(stripped);
99:     }
100: 
101:     private static String stripComments(String input) {
102:         StringBuilder out = new StringBuilder(input.length());
103:         int i = 0;
104:         while (i < input.length()) {
105:             if (i + 1 < input.length() && input.charAt(i) == '/' && input.charAt(i + 1) == '/') {
106:                 // line comment — skip to end of line
107:                 i += 2;
108:                 while (i < input.length() && input.charAt(i) != '\n') i++;
109:                 continue;
110:             }
111:             if (i + 1 < input.length() && input.charAt(i) == '/' && input.charAt(i + 1) == '*') {
112:                 // block comment — skip to */
113:                 i += 2;
114:                 while (i + 1 < input.length() && !(input.charAt(i) == '*' && input.charAt(i + 1) == '/')) i++;
115:                 i += 2; // skip */
116:                 continue;
117:             }
118:             out.append(input.charAt(i));
119:             i++;
120:         }
121:         return out.toString();
122:     }
123: 
124:     private static Map<String, String> parseJson(String input) {
125:         Map<String, String> result = new HashMap<>();
126:         int i = skipWhitespace(input, 0);
127:         if (i >= input.length() || input.charAt(i) != '{') return result;
128:         i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 1);
129: 
130:         while (i < input.length()) {
131:             if (input.charAt(i) == '}') break;
132:             if (input.charAt(i) == ',') { i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 1); continue; }
133: 
134:             // parse key
135:             if (input.charAt(i) != '"') break;
136:             int keyStart = i + 1;
137:             int keyEnd = findStringEnd(input, keyStart);
138:             if (keyEnd < 0) break;
139:             String key = input.substring(keyStart, keyEnd);
140:             i = skipWhitespace(input, keyEnd + 1);
141: 
142:             if (i >= input.length() || input.charAt(i) != ':') break;
143:             i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 1);
144: 
145:             // parse value
146:             if (i >= input.length()) break;
147:             char c = input.charAt(i);
148:             String value;
149:             if (c == '"') {
150:                 int valStart = i + 1;
151:                 int valEnd = findStringEnd(input, valStart);
152:                 if (valEnd < 0) break;
153:                 value = input.substring(valStart, valEnd);
154:                 i = skipWhitespace(input, valEnd + 1);
155:             } else if (c == 't' && input.startsWith("true", i)) {
156:                 value = "true";
157:                 i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 4);
158:             } else if (c == 'f' && input.startsWith("false", i)) {
159:                 value = "false";
160:                 i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 5);
161:             } else if (c == 'n' && input.startsWith("null", i)) {
162:                 value = "null";
163:                 i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 4);
164:             } else {
165:                 // number
166:                 int valEnd = i;
167:                 while (valEnd < input.length() && (Character.isDigit(input.charAt(valEnd))
168:                         || input.charAt(valEnd) == '.' || input.charAt(valEnd) == '-'
169:                         || input.charAt(valEnd) == '+' || input.charAt(valEnd) == 'e'
170:                         || input.charAt(valEnd) == 'E')) {
171:                     valEnd++;
172:                 }
173:                 if (valEnd == i) break;
174:                 value = input.substring(i, valEnd);
175:                 i = skipWhitespace(input, valEnd);
176:             }
177:             result.put(key, value);
178:         }
179:         return result;
180:     }
181: 
182:     private static int skipWhitespace(String s, int pos) {
183:         while (pos < s.length() && Character.isWhitespace(s.charAt(pos))) pos++;
184:         return pos;
185:     }
186: 
187:     private static int findStringEnd(String s, int start) {
188:         int i = start;
189:         while (i < s.length()) {
190:             char c = s.charAt(i);
191:             if (c == '\\') {
192:                 i += 2; // skip escaped char
193:             } else if (c == '"') {
194:                 return i;
195:             } else {
196:                 i++;
197:             }
198:         }
199:         return -1;
200:     }
201: }

(End of file - total 201 lines)
</content>

60:             System.setProperty("tfocr.settings", settingsFile.getAbsolutePath());
61:             settings = Settings.load();

66: 
67:         try {
68:             // Resolve each parameter: CLI arg > settings.jsonc > hardcoded default
69:             File resolvedOutput = outputFile != null ? outputFile
70:                     : new File(settings.getString("output.file", "output.pdf"));
71:             String resolvedTessdata = tessdataDir != null ? tessdataDir.getAbsolutePath()
72:                     : settings.getString("tessdata.dir", "./tessdata");
73:             String resolvedNative = nativeDir != null ? nativeDir.getAbsolutePath()
74:                     : settings.getString("native.dir", "native");

77: 
78:             // Create pipeline components with configured values

88:                     settings.getInt("segmenter.tileSize", 64),

90:                     settings.getInt("segmenter.inpaintRadius", 3)
91:             );

110:         } catch (Exception e) {

112:             e.printStackTrace();
113:             return 1;
114:         }

116: 
117:     private void runPipeline(File inputFile, File outputFile,
118:                              PageExtractor extractor, ImageSegmenter segmenter,
119:                              OCREngine ocrEngine, JBIG2Compressor compressor,
120:                              PDFAssembler assembler) throws IOException {
121: 
122:         // Phase 1: Extract pages as images
123:         System.out.println("  Extracting pages...");
124:         List<BufferedImage> pages;
125:         try (PageExtractor pe = extractor) {
126:             pages = pe.extractPages(inputFile);
127:         }
128: 
129:         // Phase 2: Segment each page (background normalization + binarization)
130:         System.out.println("  Segmenting pages...");
131:         List<BufferedImage> backgrounds = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());
132:         List<BufferedImage> foregroundMasks = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());
133:         for (BufferedImage page : pages) {
134:             SegmentedImage seg = segmenter.segment(page);
135:             backgrounds.add(seg.getCleanedBackground());
136:             foregroundMasks.add(seg.getForegroundMask());
137:         }
138: 
139:         // Phase 3: OCR each page
140:         System.out.println("  Running OCR...");
141:         List<PageResult> ocrResults = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());
142:         for (BufferedImage page : pages) {
143:             ocrResults.add(ocrEngine.ocr(page));
144:         }
145: 
146:         // Phase 4: Assemble searchable PDF with foreground mask overlay
147:         System.out.println("  Assembling PDF...");
148:         try (var doc = assembler.assemble(inputFile, backgrounds, foregroundMasks, ocrResults)) {
149:             doc.save(outputFile);
150:         }
151:     }
152: 
153:     public static void main(String[] args) {
154:         int exitCode = new CommandLine(new TrulyFreeOCR()).execute(args);
155:         System.exit(exitCode);
156:     }

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now I have a clear picture of the entire flow. Let me design the plan:

**Current issue**: PDFBox renders at 24-bit RGB even for text-only pages. Tesseract processes color images ~100x slower than bilevel/grayscale images.

**Proposed solution**: Add a `rendering.colorMode` setting (or similar name) that controls the color depth of images fed to Tesseract. Default to grayscale.

### Plan:

1. **Add setting to `settings.jsonc`**:
   ```jsonc
   // Color mode for images fed to Tesseract.
   //   "grayscale" = 8-bit grayscale (fast, recommended, ~50ms conversion)
   //   "rgb"       = 24-bit full color (slow, ~80s per page)
   "rendering.colorMode": "grayscale"
   ```

2. **Add a getter in `Settings.java`** — no new method needed, `getString()` already exists.

3. **Modify `OCREngine` constructor** to accept a color mode parameter:
   - Add a new parameter `String colorMode` (with default `"grayscale"`)
   - In `ocr()`, before writing PNG, convert to grayscale if `colorMode` is `"grayscale"`
   - Keep `"rgb"` as a valid option for backward compatibility

4. **Update `TrulyFreeOCR.java`** to resolve `rendering.colorMode` from settings and pass it to OCREngine.

5. **Update all OCREngine call sites** (tests, other places) to handle the new parameter:
   - `OCREngine()` no-arg constructor → defaults to `"grayscale"`
   - `OCREngine(String, String, String, String, String)` → new 5-param constructor
   - Tests using `new OCREngine()` need no change (uses default)
   - Tests using the 4-param constructor need a 5th param or update

Let me check all the places that construct OCREngine:

</details>

### Plan

**Goal**: Add `rendering.colorMode` to settings.jsonc that controls whether OCREngine converts images to grayscale before Tesseract, defaulting to grayscale for ~100x speedup.

**Changes needed**:

1. **`settings.jsonc`**: Add a new entry under the "PDF Rendering" section:
   ```jsonc
   // Color mode for images fed to Tesseract.
   //   "grayscale" = convert to 8-bit grayscale before OCR (~50ms, ~100x faster)
   //   "rgb"       = keep original 24-bit RGB (~80s per page)
   "rendering.colorMode": "grayscale"
   ```

2. **`OCREngine.java`**:
   - Add a `String colorMode` field
   - Add a new constructor overload: `OCREngine(String tessdataDir, String tesseractPath, String language, String psm, String colorMode)`
   - Keep the no-arg constructor defaulting to `"grayscale"`
   - Keep the 4-param constructor defaulting to `"grayscale"` (backward compat)
   - In `ocr()`: before `ImageIO.write()`, if `colorMode.equals("grayscale")`, convert the image to `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` using `Graphics2D.drawImage()`

3. **`TrulyFreeOCR.java`**:
   - Read `rendering.colorMode` from settings, pass it as 5th arg to OCREngine constructor
   - Add CLI option if desired (optional, user didn't ask for it)

4. **No test changes needed** — all 5 test files use `new OCREngine()` (no-arg), which defaults to `"grayscale"`. The tests would get a massive speedup automatically.

Let me present this plan to the user.

</details>

Here's the plan:

### Changes

**1. `settings.jsonc`** — add under `// ===== PDF Rendering =====`:
```jsonc
// Color mode for images fed to Tesseract.
//   "grayscale" = convert to 8-bit gray before OCR (~50ms, ~100x faster)
//   "rgb"       = keep original 24-bit RGB (~80s per page)
"rendering.colorMode": "grayscale"
```

**2. `OCREngine.java`**:
- Add `String colorMode` field
- New constructor overload: `OCREngine(..., String colorMode)`
- No-arg and 4-param constructors both default to `"grayscale"` (backward compat)
- In `ocr()`: before `ImageIO.write()`, if `colorMode == "grayscale"`, convert to `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` via:
  ```java
  BufferedImage gray = new BufferedImage(w, h, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_GRAY);
  Graphics2D g = gray.createGraphics();
  g.drawImage(image, 0, 0, null);
  g.dispose();
  // then write gray, not image
  ```

**3. `TrulyFreeOCR.java`**:
- Resolve `rendering.colorMode` from settings → `"grayscale"`
- Pass as 5th arg to `new OCREngine(...)`

### Why this works
- Grayscale conversion is **~50ms**, the same .traineddata is used, and Tesseract internally converts to grayscale anyway — so recognition quality is identical.
- **No test changes needed** — all 5 test files use `new OCREngine()` (no-arg), which defaults to `"grayscale"`. They'll get the speedup automatically.
- Setting is purely optional; `"rgb"` restores the old behavior.

Estimated speedup: **~85s/page → ~2.3s/page** (0.56s render + 1.7s PNG + 0.5s Tesseract + 0.003s parse).

Want me to proceed?

Ended: 2026-06-26T01:43:18.413Z
## User

For the "rgb" option, why does it have to be 24 bit? Why not 8-bit or 12-bit or 16-bit?

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The user is asking a good question about why the "rgb" option implies 24-bit specifically. Let me think about what happens in the pipeline.

The current situation: PDFBox's `renderImageWithDPI()` returns a `BufferedImage` with type `TYPE_INT_RGB` (24-bit, 8 bits per channel). This is the default for PDFBox rendering.

The question is: if someone chooses "rgb" mode (keeping original color), does it HAVE to be 24-bit? No, it doesn't. The BufferedImage could be:
- TYPE_INT_RGB (24-bit, 8 bits per channel) - current default from PDFBox
- TYPE_3BYTE_BGR (24-bit, 8 bits per channel) - different layout, same bit depth
- TYPE_INT_ARGB (32-bit, 8 bits per channel + alpha)
- TYPE_CUSTOM - could be anything
- TYPE_BYTE_GRAY (8-bit grayscale) - what we'd convert to
- TYPE_USHORT_GRAY (16-bit grayscale) - higher bit depth

The user's point is valid: even if we keep "color", it doesn't need to be 24-bit. PDFBox renders in 24-bit RGB, but that's an implementation detail of PDFBox.

Now, for the "rgb" option semantics: it really means "don't convert to grayscale, keep whatever PDFBox gives you". The bit depth is determined by PDFBox, not by our setting.

But I can see the user is suggesting that the naming should be clearer or that the bit depth should be independently configurable. Perhaps the setting name should be something like:

- `rendering.colorMode` with values `"grayscale"` (convert) or `"original"` (pass through)

Or maybe we should just name it based on the conversion behavior rather than implying a specific bit depth.

Let me answer the user's question directly and suggest a better name.

</details>

You're right — 24-bit is just what PDFBox happens to produce (`TYPE_INT_RGB`). There's no reason it has to be 24-bit; it could be 8-bit grayscale or even 1-bit bilevel, depending on what the renderer outputs.

The setting name `"rgb"` is misleading — it implies a specific bit depth when it really means **"don't convert, pass through whatever PDFBox renders"**. A better name would be:

```jsonc
// OCR image color mode:
//   "grayscale" = convert to 8-bit gray before feeding to Tesseract (fast, ~50ms overhead)
//   "original"  = keep PDFBox's native output (TYPE_INT_RGB, ~80s/page)
"rendering.colorMode": "original"
```

But really, is there a use case for `"original"`? The only reason to keep color would be if a downstream stage (like `ImageSegmenter` or `PDFAssembler`) needs the original pixel data — but those stages already receive the full-resolution color image from `PageExtractor` directly, independent of what's fed to Tesseract. The grayscale conversion would only affect the temp PNG written for Tesseract, not the images used elsewhere in the pipeline.

So the question is: **should we even offer an `"original"` option?** Or just hardcode grayscale conversion in `OCREngine.ocr()` and drop the setting entirely?

## User

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The user said "ok" - they're agreeing with my analysis. Since I'm in plan mode, I should confirm the simplified plan and ask if they want me to proceed with just hardcoding the grayscale conversion (no setting needed) since there's no real use case for keeping the original 24-bit RGB for Tesseract.

The plan becomes even simpler:

1. **`OCREngine.java`** - In `ocr()`, before `ImageIO.write()`, convert the BufferedImage to `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY`. No setting, no new constructor param, no config.
2. **No changes to** `settings.jsonc`, `TrulyFreeOCR.java`, or any tests.

This is the most minimal, impactful change. The grayscale conversion is a ~50ms operation that drops Tesseract time from ~80s to ~0.5s per page.

</details>

Agreed. There's no need for a setting — just hardcode the grayscale conversion in `OCREngine.ocr()`. The change is a single ~10-line addition:

**`OCREngine.java`**, in the `ocr()` method, before `ImageIO.write(image, "png", tempPng)`:

```java
// Convert to grayscale — Tesseract processes 8-bit gray ~100x faster than 24-bit RGB
if (image.getType() != BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_GRAY) {
    BufferedImage gray = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_GRAY);
    Graphics2D g = gray.createGraphics();
    g.drawImage(image, 0, 0, null);
    g.dispose();
    image = gray;
}
```

No settings, no new constructors, no test changes. Want me to implement it?

hocr also times out (exit 124). So it's not just TSV - multiple structured output formats hang on large images.

The plain text stdout format works instantly. The issue is with parsing/formatting the structured outputs.

Options:
1. **Switch to text-only output** - We lose bounding boxes, which means PDFAssembler would need to place text without precise positioning (can estimate or use full-page width)
2. **Use `txt` output** - Actually this is the same as stdout. We get sequenced text lines but no boxes.
3. **Patch the OCREngine** to request specific Tesseract config variables that reduce TSV generation time
4. **Use `--psm 6` (single uniform block)** - This might simplify the layout analysis and make TSV generation faster

Actually, looking at it: the TSV generation involves Tesseract computing and outputting per-word bounding boxes. For a large image with many words, this can be computationally expensive. The `stdout` text-only output just outputs the recognized text in reading order.

But we DO need bounding boxes for the PDF text layer. Without precise bounding boxes, the invisible text in the output PDF might not be positioned correctly over the original text.

However, looking at the PDFAssembler, it uses bounding boxes to position text. Without those, we'd need to either:
1. Not use bounding boxes (position text at generic locations)
2. Fall back to text-only mode

Actually, let me reconsider. The earlier benchmark showed TSV working in 0.655s for the moby-dick PDF. But now it hangs. Something changed between those two runs. Let me investigate what.

Wait - the 0.655s test was run with the `tsv` config file at the end, not `-c tessedit_create_tsv=1`. Let me check the difference:

Let me take a step back and think about this more carefully. The problem is fundamental: Tesseract's TSV output generation is extremely slow (or hanging) for large images when doing actual layout analysis. The text-only output (stdout) works fine in <1s.

My options are:
1. **Use Tesseract's text-only output** and generate bounding boxes ourselves (e.g., based on text length and font metrics)
2. **Use a different PSM mode** - only PSM 7 works with TSV, which is too limited
3. **Investigate and fix the Tesseract TSV slowness** - this might be a Tesseract 5.3.4 bug that we need to work around
4. **Downsample the image before OCR** - use a smaller resolution for the OCR step

Actually, option 1 is the simplest. For the PDF text layer, we don't need pixel-perfect bounding boxes. We can:
- Get the full text from Tesseract (stdout, fast)
- Split into words
- Assign approximate positions based on expected column width and line height

But that would require significant changes to the PDFAssembler.

Option 4 is also interesting: render at 300 DPI for output quality but downsample to 150 DPI for OCR. This would:
- Make Tesseract 4x faster (half the linear resolution = quarter the pixels)
- Possibly fix the TSV hang
- Slightly reduce OCR accuracy but 150 DPI is still fine

Actually, let me try PSM 3 + TSV on a 150 DPI image:

</details>

### Tool: bash

**Input:**

```json
{
  "command": "export TESSDATA_PREFIX=/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/tessdata\nexport LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/native/linux/lib\n\n# Convert pdftoppm image to proper grayscale\npython3 -c \"\nfrom PIL import Image\nim = Image.open('/tmp/tfocr-st-1.png').convert('L')\n# Convert back to RGB to keep the same mode comparison\nrgb = Image.merge('RGB', (im, im, im))\nrgb.save('/tmp/tfocr-pd-gray.png')\n# Also save as bilevel\nbw = im.point(lambda x: 0 if x < 128 else 255, '1')\nbw.save('/tmp/tfocr-pd-bw.png')\nprint('Created grayscale and bilevel versions')\n\"\n\necho \"=== pdftoppm -> grayscale RGB -> stdout ===\"\ntimeout 10 native/linux/tesseract.bin /tmp/tfocr-pd-gray.png stdout -l eng --psm 3 2>/dev/null\necho \"Exit: $?\"\n\necho \"\"\necho \"=== pdftoppm -> bilevel -> stdout ===\"\ntimeout 10 native/linux/tesseract.bin /tmp/tfocr-pd-bw.png stdout -l eng --psm 3 2>/dev/null\necho \"Exit: $?\"\n\necho \"\"\necho \"=== grayscale -> TSV ===\"\ntimeout 10 native/linux/tesseract.bin /tmp/tfocr-pd-gray.png /tmp/tfocr-pd-gray-tsv -l eng --psm 3 -c tessedit_create_tsv=1 2>/dev/null\necho \"Exit: $?\"\nwc -l /tmp/tfocr-pd-gray-tsv.tsv 2>/dev/null\nawk -F'\\t' '$1==5{print $NF}' /tmp/tfocr-pd-gray-tsv.tsv 2>/dev/null | head -5",

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Even the converted versions hang! So the content of the image matters. The PIL-generated image works, but the pdftoppm-generated image hangs.

The PIL image uses pure white background (255,255,255) with dark gray text (1,1,1).
The pdftoppm image has anti-aliased text with pure black (0,0,0) on white (255,255,255) and smooth edges.

Wait - this is the KEY difference. The PIL image has text at (1,1,1) - nearly black but not pure black. The pdftoppm image has pure black text (0,0,0). When I convert to bilevel with threshold 128, the PIL text disappears (becomes white) because 1 < 128.

Actually, let me check: the PIL text was drawn at fill=(0,0,0) but the extrema shows (1,255). So the text IS at (1,1,1), not (0,0,0). That's because PIL draws anti-aliased text and the minimum value for the anti-aliased edges is 1, but the text body IS black (0). The extrema might be misleading.

Let me look at this differently. The pdftoppm image has actual rendered PDF content - fonts, glyphs, anti-aliasing at the sub-pixel level. PDFBox's rendering creates a complex image with many different pixel values for smooth text.

The PIL image has simple text rendering with likely different anti-aliasing.

But the real question is: WHY does Tesseract hang on the pdftoppm image but work on the PIL image? This is likely a Tesseract bug related to specific image content patterns.

Given 8GB available memory and the 400x100 PIL image working fine (but 2550x3300 PIL image also working), it's unlikely to be a memory issue.

The critical question: will the grayscale conversion I added to OCREngine produce images that work like the PIL image or like the pdftoppm image?

The answer depends on how PDFBox renders images and how Java's `Graphics2D.drawImage` + `ImageIO.write(..., "png")` encodes them.

PDFBox's `renderImageWithDPI(0, 300)` returns a `BufferedImage` with type `TYPE_INT_RGB`. My grayscale conversion creates a `BufferedImage` with type `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY`. Then `ImageIO.write(gray, "png", tempPng)` writes it as a grayscale PNG.

Regardless of the root cause, the **key insight** is: the PIL-generated grayscale-like RGB image works with TSV. My grayscale conversion in OCREngine will produce a TYPE_BYTE_GRAY PNG, which should be similar to the PIL image in pixel content.

This is a Tesseract bug specific to processing 8-bit grayscale images at large resolutions. The TSV output generation has a bug or performance issue with `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` images.

My grayscale fix in OCREngine converts to `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` (8-bit) which will produce mode='L' PNGs via ImageIO. This will HANG in Tesseract!

The fix should instead:
1. Keep the image as RGB (so Tesseract doesn't hang)
2. But fill all three channels with the grayscale value (so Tesseract processes it fast as "effectively grayscale")

Or even simpler: **don't convert to TYPE_BYTE_GRAY, convert to TYPE_BYTE_INDEXED** or keep as TYPE_INT_RGB but with uniform channels.

This produces an RGB PNG with R=G=B channels. The pixel data is essentially the same as the original (grayscale) but the file format is different (24-bit vs 8-bit).

The key insight: **Tesseract with TSV hangs on 8-bit grayscale PNGs but works on 24-bit RGB PNGs** regardless of the actual pixel content.

Let me step way back and think about what my grayscale fix actually does:

1. Gets a `TYPE_INT_RGB` image from PDFBox
2. Converts to `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` 
3. Writes as 8-bit grayscale PNG via `ImageIO.write`
4. Tesseract reads the PNG and... hangs

So my fix makes things WORSE, not better.

The original code (without my fix):
1. Gets a `TYPE_INT_RGB` image from PDFBox
2. Writes as 24-bit RGB PNG via `ImageIO.write`
3. Tesseract processes it... taking 80s per page

So the original code is slow. My fix makes it hang.

The RIGHT approach is:
1. Keep the image as RGB
2. Convert the pixel data to have R=G=B (effectively grayscale color data in RGB format)
3. Write as 24-bit RGB PNG

This way Tesseract sees RGB (which it handles correctly) but the image is uniform grayscale (which it processes fast).

Let me update the fix to NOT use `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` but instead create a uniform-channel `TYPE_INT_RGB` image.

```java
if (image.getType() != BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_GRAY) {
    BufferedImage gray = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_INT_RGB);
    Graphics2D g = gray.createGraphics();
    // Draw original at reduced quality (no rendering hints) to force uniform channels
    g.drawImage(image, 0, 0, null);
    g.dispose();
    image = gray;
}
```

Wait, this doesn't help. `Graphics2D.drawImage` preserves the original colors. If the original is grayscale (R=G=B), the result will be too. But if the original has color variation (even from anti-aliasing), the result will too.

Actually, the issue was never about the pixel data. The issue was about the IMAGE TYPE. Let me verify: does a `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` BufferedImage written as PNG cause Tesseract to hang? Yes, I verified this above (mode='L' PNG hangs).

So the fix should be to convert to `TYPE_INT_RGB` (not `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY`) to avoid the Tesseract grayscale PNG bug. The speed improvement from grayscale-like content would still apply.

But wait - the original TYPE_INT_RGB images from PDFBox ALREADY take 80s per page. The PIL-generated TYPE_INT_RGB images take <1s. The difference is the pixel content, not the image type.

The difference between PDFBox and PIL images:
- PDFBox: has smooth anti-aliased edges, many unique gray values, possibly sub-pixel rendering
- PIL: simpler rendering, fewer unique pixel values

So the speed problem is about the anti-aliasing quality, not the color depth. PDFBox produces high-quality sub-pixel anti-aliased text which confuses Tesseract's layout analysis.

The solution: apply a simple threshold binarization to the image before sending to Tesseract. Convert the image to pure black-and-white (bilevel). This:
1. Removes anti-aliasing
2. Makes Tesseract very fast (bilevel is the native mode for Tesseract)
3. May slightly reduce accuracy but for clean text on white, it should be fine

Actually, let me verify: does a bilevel (1-bit) PNG work with Tesseract TSV?

It didn't show any error message. But the exit code might have been non-zero. Actually, with `2>/dev/null` the error messages were suppressed. The output was 0.655s but Tesseract probably just failed quickly because the file was gone!

This CHANGES EVERYTHING. The original 80s/page timing from the Java benchmark was REAL - Tesseract genuinely takes that long on the PDFBox images. My grayscale fix would make it WORSE (hang instead of just being slow). The only fast solutions are:
1. Use `stdout` (text-only, no bounding boxes)
2. Use a different image processing approach

Let me now think about the practical solution:

**Option A**: Use `stdout` for text and generate approximate bounding boxes
**Option B**: Downscale the image before OCR (e.g., 150 DPI), get boxes fast, then map back to 300 DPI coordinates
**Option C**: Use a completely different approach for bounding boxes

The problem with TSV output is that it's genuinely slow (minutes) on large 300 DPI images from PDFBox. The earlier tests showing 0.6s were all false positives (file didn't exist, Tesseract errored quickly).

The solution: **downscale the image before feeding to Tesseract**. Use 150 DPI for OCR, get bounding boxes fast, then scale coordinates back to 300 DPI.

Actually, an even simpler solution: the OCREngine currently writes a PNG at the full resolution. Instead, I should write the PNG at half resolution (1275×1650) for OCR, then multiply bounding boxes by 2.

Let me implement this approach:

</details>

{
  "command": "python3 -c \"\nfrom PIL import Image\nimport struct\n\n# Check PNG chunk structure\nfor name, path in [('PIL', '/tmp/tfocr-pil-rgb.png'), ('pdftoppm', '/tmp/tfocr-fresh2-1.png')]:\n    with open(path, 'rb') as f:\n        sig = f.read(8)\n        assert sig == b'\\\\x89PNG\\\\r\\\\n\\\\x1a\\\\n'\n        chunks = []\n        while True:\n            length = struct.unpack('>I', f.read(4))[0]\n            chunk_type = f.read(4)\n            data = f.read(length)\n            crc = struct.unpack('>I', f.read(4))[0]\n            chunks.append((chunk_type, length))\n            if chunk_type == b'IEND':\n                break\n        print(f'{name}: {len(chunks)} chunks')\n        for ct, l in chunks:\n            print(f'  {ct}: {l} bytes')\n        print()\n\"",
  "description": "Compare PNG chunk structure",
  "timeout": 10000,
  "workdir": "/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR"
}

- Dense PIL: 14,022 non-white pixels → TSV hangs

So the threshold is somewhere between 3,500 and 14,000 non-white pixels. The dense PIL image has 4x more text than the sparse one, and it hangs.

This suggests Tesseract's TSV output has a computational complexity issue with the amount of text content on large images. It might be O(n²) or worse in the number of text pixels or detected components.

The text-only output (stdout) works because it's just a simple text dump without generating per-character/per-word bounding boxes.

Given this understanding:
1. TSV output is broken for realistic document pages at 300 DPI
2. Text-only output (stdout/txt) works fine
3. My grayscale fix won't help (TSV is fundamentally slow, not color-dependent)

Actually, let me just implement the grayscale fix properly and then measure performance. The grayscale fix alone gives ~35% speedup which is meaningful. If the user wants more, we can consider downscaling.

But first, I need to fix my grayscale conversion. The `TYPE_BYTE_GRAY` causes Tesseract to be slow and was shown to hang in some cases. Let me use a safer approach: keep the image as `TYPE_INT_RGB` but apply a grayscale color conversion (all three channels equal). This avoids the Tesseract grayscale PNG bug.

Actually, looking at the data:
- Original RGB (TYPE_INT_RGB, type=1) → TSV 34.5s
- Grayscale (TYPE_BYTE_GRAY, type=10) → TSV 22.3s

The grayscale version is FASTER (22.3s vs 34.5s). And it produced correct TSV output (3993 bytes). So the TYPE_BYTE_GRAY conversion does NOT cause a hang for PDFBox images on simple-text.pdf.

The earlier "hanging" tests were with pdftoppm images on the specific simple-text.pdf page, which had different rendering characteristics. The OCREngine uses PDFBox, not pdftoppm.

So my grayscale fix IS helpful:

All 20 unit tests pass. The grayscale fix is working — page times dropped from ~85s to ~40-80s (~20-40% improvement).

The core issue is simply that **Tesseract is slow at 300 DPI on this hardware** regardless of color mode or output format — recognition itself takes 35-80s per dense page. The larger evaluation corpora (50/200/500 pages) will take hours.

If you want to speed this up further, the next step would be to **downscale images before OCR** (e.g., OCR at 150 DPI, then scale bounding boxes back to 300 DPI), which would give roughly 4x faster throughput at a slight accuracy cost. Alternatively, we can just proceed with the current speed.
